segments.s242-g33: ز پرده درآمد is a compact verbal phrase: ز (from/away from) + پرده (curtain) + درآمد (entered/came forth). درآمد here functions as the past action ‘came out/entered (into view)’. یکی پرده‌دار follows and identifies the newcomer: یکی = 'a certain / one' placed before the noun پرده‌دار (curtain-keeper/chamberlain), so the whole segment means 'from the curtain came a certain curtain-keeper.'
segments.s242-g34: به نزدیک سالار شد هوشیار combines a directional phrase and a state-change. به نزدیک (toward/near) + سالار (the commander) marks the goal/location. شد هوشیار uses شد as a past/resultative 'became' with the predicative adjective هوشیار (alert/watchful). The implied subject is the پرده‌دار from the previous segment: 'he approached the commander and became alert.'
segments.s242-g35: که introduces a relative/nominal clause ('that' / 'who'). بر در بپایند is an older-form verb phrase meaning 'stand at the gate' or 'take up position at the door/gate': بر (on/at) + در (gate) + بپایند (3rd‑person plural present/habitual of 'to stand / to station oneself'). ارمانیان is the subject at the end (Armenians), so the clause reads 'who/that the Armenians stand at the gate.'
segments.s242-g36: سر مرز is a stock idiom meaning 'at the border/frontier' (سر = top/edge; مرز = border). It is followed by two place-names joined by و: توران (Turan) و ایرانیان (the Iranians), so the phrase pinpoints the location: 'at the frontier of Turan and the Iranians.'
segments.s242-g37: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle common in epic Persian meaning 'continually/still' and marks ongoing or habitual action. راه جویند is a present/habitual 3rd‑person plural verb phrase ('they seek the way' / 'they look for a path'). نزدیک شاه is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'near the king' modifying جویند. Together: 'They are continually seeking a way near the king.'
segments.s242-g38: ز راه دراز آمده is a participial/clausal modifier: ز (from) + راه دراز (a long road / long way) + آمده (having come / arrived). دادخواه is the noun that this clause qualifies or introduces: 'a seeker of justice / petitioner.' So the whole segment means 'a petitioner who has come from a long road' or more loosely 'a petitioner has come from afar.'
segments.s242-g39: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' سالار هشیار is the noun phrase 'the alert/awake commander' (adjective after noun in this position). بشنید is the simple past 'he heard.' رفت is the following past verb 'he went'—a common narrative sequence: 'When the alert commander heard, he went.' The subject of both verbs is سالار هشیار.
segments.s242-g40: به نزدیک خسرو is the directional phrase 'toward/near Khosrow' (به + نزدیک + proper name). خرامید is a past verb meaning 'he strode / walked proudly.' تفت here is a short, emphatic poetic tag following the verb; in context it functions as an adverbial/intensifying particle modifying the manner of the stride ('with pride / proudly'). The whole segment reads 'he went up to Khosrow and strode proudly.'
segments.s242-g257: This is a compact poetic clause: 'پری چهره بینی' = 'you see (pôle) fairy-faced [ones]'. بینی is the classical 2nd-person present of دیدن used impersonally ('one sees' / 'you see'). The sequence places the adjective/epithet (پری‌چهره) before the implicit noun and the verb بینی; 'همه دشت و کوه' follows as the location 'across all plains and mountains'.
segments.s242-g258: ز هر سو = 'from every side'; سو here means 'side' and هر quantifies it. نشسته is a past-participial/adjectival form meaning 'seated' (state), and به شادی marks the state 'in joy/celebration' with به as a prepositional marker. گروه is the noun 'company/group' and completes the image of people sitting in festivity.
segments.s242-g259: This is a question word order: کجا 'where' follows the name for emphasis: 'منیژه، کجا؟' followed by an appositive 'دخت افراسیاب' ('the daughter of Afrasiab'). The noun phrase functions as the interrogative subject/topic.
segments.s242-g260: درفشان کند is a verbal phrase where کند is the 3rd-person singular of کردن ('makes/does') and درفشان (here 'shining, glittering') describes the result; باغ is the object 'garden', and چون آفتاب is a simile 'like the sun'. Literally: 'she makes the garden sparkle like the sun.'
segments.s242-g261: A verbless, descriptive nominal phrase: 'همه دخت توران' = 'all the maidens of Turan' followed by پوشیده‌روی ('veiled-faced/modest') as their attributive quality. In classical Persian poetry such lists omit a copula and string noun + adjective phrases.
segments.s242-g262: Parallel descriptive phrases: 'همه سرو بالا' uses سرو metaphorically for tall, elegant figures ('tall beauties/cypresses') with بالا as the attribute 'lofty/high'; then 'همه مشک موی' = 'all with musk hair' (i.e., hair with the fragrance of musk). Again no verb is expressed; these are coordinated epithets.
segments.s242-g263: A continuation of the catalog: 'همه رخ پر از گل' = 'all faces full of flowers' where پر از is the fixed phrase 'full of'. The following 'همه چشم خواب' uses خواب in the adjectival sense 'sleepy/asleep' to describe the eyes. These are noun + adjective/state phrases without an expressed verb.
segments.s242-g264: 'همه لب پر از می‌به' = 'all lips full of mi-beh' where می‌به is an archaic/poetic drink (quince-wine); پر از marks 'full of'. 'بوی گلاب' ('the scent of rosewater') is appended to qualify the lips with rosewater fragrance. The whole is a sensory description in series.
segments.s242-g369: به پرده درآمد is a verbal phrase meaning "entered into the curtain/tent" where به + noun marks motion into/onto and درآمد (here functioning as a past action) means "came in/entered." چو introduces a simile following the verb, so چو سرو بلند = "like a tall cypress." Word order places the prepositional phrase before the verb and the simile after it.
segments.s242-g370: میانش = میان + enclitic ـش "her middle/at her waist." به here functions adverbially/introduces the instrument/attaching matter ("with/ having ") before زرین کمر. کرده is a past participle (literally "having made/put") modifying بند; بند here means a band/fastening. The phrase thus describes a golden belt bound about her middle.
segments.s242-g371: منیژه بیامد is a simple past clause "Manijeh came." گرفتَش is گرفت + enclitic ـش, a verb meaning "seized/took (her/him)." به بر is an idiomatic phrase meaning "into the embrace / to the bosom" (به marking direction/taking into and بر here functioning as the locus of embrace).
segments.s242-g372: گشاد here has the sense "loosened/let loose" (not merely 'wide'); از introduces the source "from" and میانش = "her middle/waist." کیانی کمر is a genitive-like phrase "the Kayanian (royal) girdle/belt." Together: she loosened the royal girdle from about her waist.
segments.s242-g373: بپرسیدش here is a past-tense verb phrase meaning "they asked her" (the verb + enclitic object). از introduces the topic/contents of the questioning ("about/concerning"), so از راه و رنج دراز = "about the road and the long hardship."
segments.s242-g374: This is an indirect question compressed into one line: the first که functions as a question marker/"who," با تو = "with you," the second که introduces the subordinate clause "who came" (که آمد), and به جنگ گراز = "to the fight/battle with the boar." Word order: "Who (that) with you who came to fight the boar?" → natural English: "Who came with you to fight the boar?"
segments.s242-g375: چرا این‌چنین begins the question "why so/why thus." روی, بالا, برز are a triad of descriptive nouns (face, loftiness/height, exaltation) enumerated to describe appearance or bearing; the sequence intensifies the addresser's surprise at the person's proud or lofty aspect.
segments.s242-g376: برنجانی is from برنجیدن "to be offended/resent/angry" and here addresses the addressee as "are you offended/angry." ای is the vocative "O," خوب‌چهره is an epithet "beautiful-faced/fair-faced." به گرز literally "with a mace" (به marking instrument/means). Together this is a vocative question: "O fair-faced one, are you thus offended with a mace?" (i.e. bearing a weapon in anger).
segments.s242-g377: بشستند is past tense, 3rd-person plural (‘they washed’). پایش is پای + enclitic pronoun ش ‘his foot’, functioning as the direct object. به + مشک و گلاب uses به instrumentally/with (here 'with musk and rosewater'), so the phrase means 'they washed his foot with musk and rosewater.' Word order places the verb before its object, a common poetic/classical order.
segments.s242-g378: گرفتند is past 3rd-plural of گرفتن; here it functions idiomatically with an infinitive to mean 'they began/undertook'. زان پس is a temporal sequence ('from that, then/after that'). به + خوردن indicates purpose/infinitive ('to eat'), and شتاب is a noun 'haste' placed after the infinitive phrase: 'they (took) then to eating (with) haste' → 'they hurried to eat.'
segments.s242-g379: نهادند is past 3rd-plural of نهادن ('they placed' / 'they set'). خوان و خورش are coordinated direct objects ('banquet/table and cooked dishes'). گونه گون is an adjectival pair meaning 'of various kinds' or 'many-colored' placed after the noun it qualifies (typical in Persian poetic word order).
segments.s242-g380: همی is an archaic/progressive adverbial marker meaning 'continually / kept on' and here gives a durative flavour to ساختند (past plural 'they made/prepared'). از introduces the source/reasonal phrase, and گمانی فزون ('gaman-i fuzun') is a genitive-like phrase meaning 'more than imagined' / 'exceeding expectation' (گمانی = supposition/expectation, فزون = abundant/excessive). Together: 'they kept making (more) beyond what one might have supposed.'
segments.s242-g381: نشستنگه is a compound noun 'place/seat for sitting'. رود here is the name of a string instrument (rūd/barbat-like) and functions as the object or instrumental noun linked to می‌ساختند; می‌ساختند is past progressive/form with the prefix می- indicating an ongoing action in narrative past ('they were making/they played/produced'). The construction places the noun (instrument) before the verb, common in poetic sequences describing music.
segments.s242-g382: ز is the poetic form of از and introduces the agent/source ('from the strangers'). بیگانه is 'stranger/foreign', and خیمه is 'tent'. بپرداختند is past 3rd-plural of پرداختن with the prefix بـ (poetic variant) and here means 'they attended to / took care of / dealt with' (or in some contexts 'they paid [tribute]'). Grammatically it completes the clause: 'and they attended to the strangers' tents.'
segments.s242-g383: پرستندگان is the plural agent noun 'worshippers/devotees'. ایستاده is the adjective/participle 'standing', placed after the noun as a predicate/adjectival phrase. به پای is a prepositional phrase 'at the feet (of)' or 'by the feet', where به marks the relation and پای is the noun 'foot/feet'. Together: 'worshippers, standing at his feet...'.
segments.s242-g384: This is a noun cluster listing musical elements and a performer. ابا in this position is an archaic/poetic word probably representing a vocal/aural term (compare آوا 'sound/voice'); if taken as such it heads the list 'voice/singing with barbat and harp...'. بربط and چنگ are instrument names (barbat/lute and harp). رامش سرای is a compound where رامش (a soothing/solace noun) combines with سرای (one who composes/sings) to mean 'a minstrel/singer of solace' or 'composer of soothing songs'. Grammatically these items form a coordinated descriptive list modifying the scene of worshippers standing.
segments.s242-g425: به marks the beneficiary or general direction ('for/to men'). ز introduces the following noun phrase 'هر گونه کار' ('from/in every kind of affair/situation'). آیدا is a poetic/present‑tense verb form meaning 'comes/occurs' (a metrically extended/archaic form of آید) and completes the clause.
segments.s242-g426: گهی ... گه ... are correlative adverbs meaning 'sometimes ... sometimes ...' (alternate occurrences). بزم and کارزار are coordinated nouns ('feast/banquet' and 'battle/field of battle') and آیدا (repeated) is the same present‑tense verb 'comes/occurs', so the phrase reads 'sometimes feast and sometimes battle comes'.
segments.s242-g427: ز هر خرگهی is a prepositional phrase 'from every chamber/den' (ز used poetically). گل‌رخی functions as a descriptive noun/adjective 'the rosy‑cheeked (one)' and خواستند is simple past, 3rd‑person plural 'they wanted/sought', so the clause means 'they sought the rosy‑cheeked from every chamber.'
segments.s242-g428: به + material (دیبای رومی) gives the manner/garment ('in Roman brocade'), and بیاراستند is past tense, 3rd‑person plural of بیاراستن 'to adorn/dress'. The word order places the material before the verb: 'they adorned (her) in Roman brocade.'
segments.s242-g429: پری چهرگان is a compound description: پری ('fairy/fair') modifies چهرگان ('faces' / 'appearances') to mean 'the fair‑faced ones' or 'fair maidens.' رود is the noun 'river' and برداشتند is past, 3rd‑person plural of برداشتن 'they took/they brought (away/to)', so the clause reads 'they brought/led the fair‑faced ones to the river.'
segments.s242-g430: به شادی is a prepositional/time phrase 'in joy/for celebration.' همه روز is a temporal expression 'the whole day,' and بگذاشتند is past 3rd‑plural of گذاشتن with a prefixed ب- (here idiomatically 'they spent' time). Together: 'they spent the whole day in joy.'
segments.s242-g233: کنون is a sentence-initial temporal adverb 'now'. گفتنی‌ها is a plural noun phrase 'things to be said' (گفتنی + ها) placed before the verb as a topic. بگویم is the first-person present/subjunctive form of گفتن used here with a futurate/intent meaning 'I will say / I shall tell'; ترا is the object pronoun 'to you' written here split into ت (clitic object) + را (direct-object marker). Word order is topicalized object(s) before the verb, typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s242-g234: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. من is the subject 'I'. چندگه is an adverb meaning roughly 'for a while / how long' (an indefinite/temporal adverb). بوده‌ام is the compound past (past participle بوده + auxiliary ام) yielding a perfect/experience sense 'I have been' / 'I was'. ایدرا is an archaic adverb meaning 'here / to this place' and sits at the end of the clause.
segments.s242-g235: چه ... چه ... here functions as a correlative/categorical marker 'whether... (or)...' rather than a simple interrogative. با is the preposition 'with' and repeats before each group. رستم, گیو and گژدهم are proper names joined by و 'and'. The phrase lists companions/associates and is syntactically a coordinate series governed by the omitted verb in the next line.
segments.s242-g236: The same correlative pattern continues: چه introduces the alternative, با is 'with'. طوس here is the hero Tous (proper name). نوذر is the proper name Nowzar (not the common noun), and the final چه با گستهم gives the alternative 'or with Gostaham' (گستهم is an archaic proper name). The preposition با takes these names as its objects.
segments.s242-g237: چه مایه is an exclamatory phrase 'what a quantity/measure of'. مایه here has the sense of 'substance/measure' applied to هنرها 'skills/arts' (plural). بر + ین together give برین 'on this' (here split as the preposition بر and the demonstrative ین/'این' = 'this'), followed by پهن دشت 'broad plain' (adjective ثم noun). The phrase is a topical/exclamatory noun phrase describing the extent of deeds.
segments.s242-g238: که introduces the relative/explanatory clause 'that'. کردیم is the past-tense plural 'we did' / 'we accomplished'. و is 'and'. گردون is the poetic word for the sky/heaven often used metonymically for 'fate' here, and بران is an archaic adverb 'thereon/over them' (literally 'on that/there'). بر گذشت is the verb گذشت 'passed' with the prepositional/archaic particle, so the clause says 'and the sky/fate passed over them.' The verb appears at the end, as typical.
segments.s242-g239: کجا is used rhetorically as 'whence' / 'from where'. نام ما 'our name' (reputation) is the theme. زان 'from that' (ز + آن) introduces origin 'from that arose', and برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth'. بلند is a predicative adjective 'lofty/high' describing how the name arose. The phrase asks rhetorically where their great renown came from.
segments.s242-g240: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the presence of' (به + نزدیک). خسرو is the proper name Khosrow (the king), the locus of honor. شدیم is the past-tense plural 'we became', and ارجمند is the adjective 'esteemed/ honored' functioning as predicate complement: 'we became honored/esteemed at the king's court.'
segments.s242-g129: This clause has a poetic inverted word order: the indirect-object phrase بدو (to him) comes before the verb گفت (said), and the speaker name خسرو follows the verb (typical older/epic Persian ordering). که‌ای is a vocative/attention formula (archaic spelling of که ای) introducing direct address, and پر هنر is a compound adjective meaning “full of skill” (پر = “full (of) + noun” without an ezafe).
segments.s242-g130: This is an imperative/adverbial instruction: همیشه (always) modifies the following predicate; به پیش + بدیها is a prepositional phrase meaning “in the presence of / before evils” (پیش = “in front of / before”), and سپر is the predicate noun 'shield' — i.e. ‘be a shield (against evil).’ The verb ‘be’ is implied by the imperative sense of the instruction.
segments.s242-g131: This rhetorical question/statement uses کجا ('where') idiomatically to mean 'ever / anywhere'; کسی را with the object-marker را is the indefinite person 'anyone'; چون تو is a comparative 'like you', کهتر is the comparative adjective 'inferior', and بود is the copula in past form — overall: 'Was there anyone more inferior than you?'.
segments.s242-g132: ز (archaic/poetic preposition from/of) introduces the cause/object 'the enemy'; بترسد is a present/subjunctive-form verb here with a generalizing sense 'fears / would fear'; سبکسر is a predicate adjective 'rash, light-headed', and بود is the copula — roughly: 'He who fears the enemy is rash.'
segments.s242-g133: به گرگین marks the indirect object ('to Gorgin'); میلاد is the speaker’s name (here a proper name; the listed senses treat it as a common noun), and گفت is the past verb 'said'. آنگهی is an adverb 'then / thereupon' giving temporal sequence; the whole clause means 'Then Milad said to Gorgin...'.
segments.s242-g134: که introduces reported speech ('that'); بیژن is the subject; به توران is the prepositional phrase 'to Turan'; نداند is the negative imperfect/past-type form of 'to know' meaning 'did not know' in this context; رهی is an archaic/poetic form referring to the road/way and functions as the object of نداند ('did not know the way').
segments.s242-g135: This is an imperative instruction: تو (you) is the subject, با او ('with him') marks accompaniment, and برو is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'go'. تا introduces the extent 'as far as / up to', and سر آب‌بند is a compound meaning 'the head/top of the embankment/dam' (سر = 'top/head', آب‌بند = 'dam').
segments.s242-g136: همش here means 'all of it / throughout the whole (distance)' modifying the imperative باش ('be'); راهبر is the predicate noun 'guide'. The coordinating و ('and') links the next phrase, هم is 'also', and یارمند is a noun meaning 'ally/helper/companion' — together: 'be guide all the way and also be his companion.'
segments.s242-g241: جشنگاهست is a fused poetic form meaning 'there is a festival-place' (جشنگاه + است). یکی at the start is the indefinite 'a certain/one' (here introducing the existence of such a place). ز ایدر نه دور literally 'from here not far' = 'not far from here' (نه before دور is a negation making 'not far'). The phrase word order is typical of classical Persian: indefinite + predicate + prepositional phrase.
segments.s242-g242: به دو روزه راه اندر آید بتور describes how the place is reached. به + time/measure phrase (به دو روزه = 'by two days' / 'a two-days' [journey]') modifies راه ('road, route'), اندر آید is a present-tense narrative verb 'comes/arrives' used impersonally here ('is reached/one comes into'); بتور is a modal adverb 'in this manner/so'. Note روزه here is an archaic/poetic form of 'روز' ('day'), so its sense is not the ordinary 'fast'.
segments.s242-g243: یکی at the start again has the indefinite 'one/a certain'. دشت بینی uses بینی as the present/impersonal 'one sees' (poetic 2nd-person-form used generically: 'you/one see(s)'). The following صفت phrase همه سبز و زرد ('all green and yellow') is a description of that plain; adjective(s) follow the noun in poetic order.
segments.s242-g244: کزو = 'from which' (referring back to the plain). شاد گردد is a compound where گردد (گشت) is a present/subjunctive-like verb meaning 'becomes' or 'is made', so 'is made joyful / becomes glad'. دل رادمرد is the patient/subject 'the heart of the noble/brave man' placed after the verb in inverted poetic order.
segments.s242-g245: This is a coordinated list: همه بیشه و باغ و آب روان = 'all [is] grove and garden and flowing water'. آب روان is a noun + postpositive adjective structure common in poetry, meaning 'running/flowing water' (روان here = 'flowing').
segments.s242-g246: یکی جایگه = 'one place/spot' (indefinite). از در پهلوان is a prepositional phrase 'from the door/court of the champion' where در is used in the sense 'door/gate/court' (i.e. the hero's hall/presence). Together: 'a spot at the hero's door/court.'
segments.s242-g247: زمین پرنیان uses پرنیان (postpositive adjective) to mean 'silken' or 'covered with silk'—a poetic hyperbole for richly carpeted/velvet ground. هوا مشکبوی likewise places the adjective after the noun: 'the air is musk-scented' (مشکبوی = 'musk-scented').
segments.s242-g248: گلابست is an adjectival/predicative description 'it is rose-scented'. گویی here is an archaic/modal particle meaning 'it seems / one might say' (not the modern noun 'conversation'); مگر in this context introduces a comparison 'as if' or 'as though' (the listed senses do not include this exact use, so it's treated as a comparative particle). آب جوی = 'water of a stream' (جوی = stream). The clause is a simile: 'so rose-scented that one might think it the water of a stream.'
segments.s242-g321: بپرسش is an imperative/optative form meaning 'ask!' (a command or exhortation). که introduces a subordinate clause 'who/that', چوْن here means 'how', آمدی is 2nd-person past/Perfect 'you came'. ایدرا is an archaic/poetic item (here left untranslated as a particle/place-name) that ties to the following clause; the whole segment = 'Ask who you are, how you came (here)'.
segments.s242-g322: نیایی is here the negative/imperative sense 'do not come' (an older verbal form). ب is the prepositional element 'to/into' (attached to the following word in normal prose). دین is the element that with ب yields 'to this' (in the manuscript split as ب + دین). بزمگاه is the noun 'assembly/banquet-place'. اندرا is a proper/name or vocative particle (here addressed), so the clause reads roughly 'do not come to this assembly, Andra.'
segments.s242-g323: پریزاده‌ای‌گر is a compound/archaic conditional expression meaning 'if (you) are fairy‑born' or 'one born of a pari'; گر is the conditional particle 'if/when' fused here. سیاوشیا is an adjectival/relational form 'Siyâvash‑like' or 'of Siyâvash's line'. Together this is a conditional topic: 'If you are fairy‑born or of Siyâvash...'.
segments.s242-g324: که introduces the content/result clause 'that/for which'. دلها is the plural 'hearts' (subject). به marks the relation 'toward/with/regarding' and combines with مهرت (مهر + ـت, 'your love' or 'love') to mean 'with/for your love'. همی is the old continuous/progressive particle ('still/continually') and جوشیا is a present/participial adjective 'boiling/fermenting' describing the hearts. The clause = 'that hearts continually boil with your love.'
segments.s242-g325: وگر is the conditional 'if/and if'. خاست is an archaic past/preterite 'arose/began'. اندر 'in/within' governs جهان 'the world'. رستخیز is a heavy noun meaning 'resurrection' (often Day of Rising) or an uprising; here it denotes a great upheaval. The segment states the hypothetical setting: 'If a resurrection/uprising arose in the world...'.
segments.s242-g326: که again introduces the subordinate clause. بفروختی is an old verbal form with conditional/past flavor here ('if you should sell / if you sold / if you were to set on sale'), used elliptically — in context it means something like 'if you were to deal with' or 'if you were to kindle/dispatch'. آتش is 'fire', مهر is 'love/affection', and تیز is the adjective 'keen/sharp'. Grammatically this is a conditional result: 'if you were to (so) sell/ignite the keen fire of love.'
segments.s242-g327: که introduces the explanatory/result clause. من is the first‑person pronoun 'I'. سالیان is a durative noun 'for years' (adverbial). اندرین is a fused prepositional + demonstrative 'in this'. مرغزار is the location noun 'meadow'. The phrase positions the speaker in time and place: 'that I, for years, in this meadow ...'.
segments.s242-g328: همی is the old continuous/emphatic particle 'always/continually'. جشن is 'celebration/festival' (the object). سازم is the 1st‑person present/future verb 'I make/perform' (here 'I will hold'). به هر نوبهار is a temporal phrase 'at every new spring' (نوبهار = new spring/Nowruz). Together: 'I will continually hold a festival here at every spring.'
segments.s242-g201: به دلش اندر آمد is an idiomatic past construction: آمد (past 'came') with اندر + به + دلش = 'it came into his heart' (i.e. a feeling occurred to him). ازان کار درد is a genitive phrase 'pain/sorrow from that affair' where ازان = 'from that' modifies کار 'affair' and درد 'pain' is the head noun.
segments.s242-g202: ز بدنامی خویش is a causal/agent phrase 'because of his own ill‑repute' (ز = 'because of/from'; بدنامی = 'infamy/notoriety'; خوی + ش = خویش 'his own'). ترسید is the main verb in past tense 'he feared', and مرد, placed after the verb in poetic order, is the subject 'the man'.
segments.s242-g203: دلش را is the definite direct object marked by را ('his heart'); بپیچید is past tense 'he twisted/turned' (here 'turned' figuratively). آهرمنا (Ahriman) follows the verb and functions as the agent/subject in the poetic word order: 'Ahriman turned his heart.'
segments.s242-g204: بد انداختن is an infinitival/noun phrase 'to cast/do evil' or 'evil casting'; کرد is the past tense of 'do/make' taking that infinitive phrase as its object ('he caused/made an evil casting'). با introduces the instrument/associate 'with', and بیژنا is treated as a proper name or agent involved in that evil action.
segments.s242-g205: سگالش is a rare/uncertain noun with the possessive clitic -ش 'his' (here 'his commission/command' or 'his message'). چنین ... چنین frames the clause adverbially 'thus/so ... so'; بد نوشته is 'badly written' with نوشته functioning as the past participle 'written' modified by بد 'badly'.
segments.s242-g206: نکرد is the negative past 'did not do' (here 'did not [remember]'); ایچ = 'nothing/not at all' intensifies the negation; یاد از جهان‌آفرین = 'remembrance of the Creator' where از = 'of/concerning' links یاد to جهان‌آفرین ('the world‑creator'). The whole clause: 'he did not at all remember the Creator.'
segments.s242-g207: کسی 'someone/anyone' combined with کو (classical/relative particle) introduces the protasis 'whoever/anyone who...'. به ره بر کند is a verbal phrase 'does/places [something] on the way' used idiomatically here as 'obstructs the road' (کند = 'does/makes', بر = directional/privative prefix 'on/upon', به ره = 'on the road'). ژرف چاه is the object 'a deep pit/well'.
segments.s242-g208: سزد‌گر is an impersonal/agentive noun 'one who deems it fitting' or read as 'it is fitting'; نهد is the verb 'puts/places' (used here in the impersonal sense 'it should be put'); در بن چاه = 'into the bottom (بن) of the pit (چاه)'; گاه is a temporal/occasional adverb ('occasion'/'place' in classical usage) but here completes the phrase 'into the bottom of the pit.' Together: 'it is fitting to place him in the bottom of a deep pit.'
segments.s242-g65: به دندان is a prepositional/instrumental phrase meaning “with/to the tusks” (here instrumental). چو پیلان = “like elephants,” where چو is the simile marker and پیلان is the archaic/plural form of پیل (elephant). به تن همچو کوه repeats the instrumental idea (به + تن “to/at the body”) with همچو 'like' before the simile noun کوه; the two phrases together describe appearance (tusks like elephants, bodies like mountains).
segments.s242-g66: وزیشان is وز (force/weight) plus the possessive -شان = “their force/weight.” شده is the past participle/verb “has become / has made,” and شهر ارمان ستوه is the resulting predicate: شهر (city) + ارمان (here a proper name — not one of the listed noun senses) + ستوه (weary). The construction means “their force has made the city Arman weary.”
segments.s242-g67: هم ... و هم is the correlative “both ... and ...”. از introduces the source (“from”), so هم از چارپایان و هم کشتمند = “both from the four‑footed animals and from the killers/deadly ones.” چارپایان is the plural “quadrupeds”; کشتمند is an older word meaning those that kill / deadly beings.
segments.s242-g68: ازیشان = از + شان “from them.” به ما بر = “upon/against us” (بر = upon/toward). چه مایه گزند is an interrogative: چه مایه “what sort/amount (of)” + گزند “harm,” asking what harm they can inflict. Note the object (گزند) follows the measure phrase.
segments.s242-g69: درختان is the topic “trees.” کشتن is the verbal noun/infinitive “killing / felling,” here functioning as the object of نداریم (“we do not have”) — thus کشتن نداریم یاد = “we have no recollection of killing.” یاد is the noun “memory/notice.” Word order is topical: the topic (trees) is fronted, then the nominalized verb and the negated possession.
segments.s242-g70: به دندان here again is instrumental “with their teeth/tusks”; به دو نیمه uses به + measure (“into/toward two halves”) with نیمه “half.” کردند is the simple past plural “they did / they made,” the verb taking the omitted object (the trees) from the previous clause. شاد is a postverbal adjective/adverb “gladly/happily.”
segments.s242-g71: نیاید is the classical negative-present/subjunctive form “does not / will not come.” به دندانشان = به + دندان + -شان “to/at their tusks.” سنگ سخت is the noun + adjective “a hard stone.” Together: “A hard stone will not come to their tusks.”
segments.s242-g72: مگرمان (مگر + enclitic) introduces the exception clause “unless” (or “except that”). به یکباره is the adverbial “all at once / suddenly,” and برگشت بخت is a noun phrase “the reversal/turning of fortune” (برگشت = reversal/return, بخت = fortune). The clause means roughly “unless, all at once, fortune is reversed (for us).”
segments.s242-g113: جوان گرچه دانا بود is a concessive clause: گرچه introduces 'although'; دانا is an adjective 'wise/learned' and بود is the past copula 'was'. The following با گهر is an instrumental prepositional phrase ('with/by a gem') that links to the verb in the next segment; word order is poetic, so the modifier با گهر can precede the main verb.
segments.s242-g114: آبی here is an adjective ('blue') which in poetry is separated from the noun it qualifies (گهر). آزمایش نگیرد is a negative present verb phrase: آزمایش is the object 'test' and نگیرد (does not take/undergo) is third-person singular present negative — so 'does not undergo a test.' هنر at the end is the subject 'skill/art' (Persian often places the subject at the end).
segments.s242-g115: بد و نیک 'bad and good' are coordinated adjectives; هر گونه 'every kind' is the noun phrase they modify. باید کشید is an impersonal obligation: باید 'must/ought' plus کشید (infinitival/derived verb form of کشیدن meaning 'to bear/endure' in this context) yields 'one must endure.' The subject is general/impersonal and omitted.
segments.s242-g116: ز here means 'from/of' (poetic Z-), هر 'every' modifies تلخ و شوری ('bitter and salty' — figuratively bitter and sharp hardships). بباید is an older/poetic form of باید used with the following verb; چشید (from چشیدن) means 'to taste' but figuratively 'to experience.' The clause is again impersonal: 'one must taste/experience every bitterness and salt.'
segments.s242-g117: به راهی 'toward/onto a road' is followed by a relative clause که هرگز نرفتی ('which you have never gone') — نرفتی is past tense, 2nd person singular. مپوی is a negative imperative (classical/poetic) from پوییدن: m- prefix + پوی = 'do not go/seek.' Together: 'Do not follow a road which you have never traveled.'
segments.s242-g118: بر شاه 'toward the king' is a prepositional phrase; خیره is a participial/adjectival phrase 'staring' that describes the manner. مبر is a negative imperative 'do not take' and آبروی (reputation/honor) is the object placed after the verb. The construction means 'do not, by staring at the king, take away his honor.'
segments.s242-g119: ز گفت پدر is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/at the father's saying' (ز + گفت used as 'the saying/words'); پس 'then/thereupon' links to the result. برآشفت is a past verb 'became angry/upset' and سخت is an adverb 'greatly/very' modifying it. The clause: 'At his father's words, then, he became very angry.'
segments.s242-g120: جوان بود is the simple past copula 'he was young.' The following coordinated adjectives و هشیار و پیروز بخت describe him: هشیار 'alert/awake' and پیروز بخت is a compound meaning 'fortunate/victorious in fortune' (پیروز modifying بخت = 'with victorious/fortunate luck').
segments.s242-g289: 'زنیم' is a 1st-person-plural volitive/subjunctive ('let us ...' / 'we should ...') derived from زدن; here it functions as an exhortation 'let us (speak/act)'. 'آنگهی' is an adverb ('then, thereupon') marking the time after the exhortation. 'رای هشیارتر' is a noun + comparative adjective phrase ('a wiser / more prudent counsel') with رای (counsel/plan) followed by the comparative هشیارتر.
segments.s242-g290: شود is the present/subjunctive or stative verb 'become' (here 'will become' / 'grows'). دل is the subject 'the heart / mind'. ز دیدار is a causal/ablative phrase 'from / by (the) sight / meeting' giving the reason, and بیدارتر is the comparative adjective 'more awake / more alert' predicated of دل (i.e. 'the heart becomes more awake'). Word order (verb before subject) is common in poetry.
segments.s242-g291: به + [person] (به گنجور) marks the indirect object 'to the treasurer / to the treasury-officer'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (introducing reported/quoted matter). آن کلاه بزر is the demonstrative + noun + adjective 'that great hat' which serves as the topic/object of what is said.
segments.s242-g292: که is a relative/complementizer 'which / that' introducing the subordinate clause describing the hat. در بزمگه 'in the banquet-hall' is a locative prepositional phrase; بر ... نهادم is the verb phrase 'I placed upon' where بر + noun (سر) indicates placement 'on (the) head' and نهادم is the 1st-person past 'I placed / I set'. The final به سر is the goal/position 'on (my) head' (poetic word order places these elements around the verb).
segments.s242-g293: که introduces a relative clause describing the object to be brought: روشن شدی (shodi) 'became bright / shone' is a perfective/result verb (here 'it shone / became bright'); ز (az) + (او) 'from it' indicates source or cause 'by/through it'; همه بزمگاه 'the whole banquet-hall' is the affected location ('the entire hall was illuminated'). In short, 'that which made the whole hall bright.'
segments.s242-g294: بیاور is an imperative 'bring (it/them)'. که here introduces the reason/explanation 'for / because'. ما را is a dative/beneficiary phrase 'for us' (with را marking the object), and کنونست گاه is a nominal predicate 'now is the time' (کنونست = 'it is now', گاه = 'time/occasion'), giving the motivation for the command.
segments.s242-g295: همان is the demonstrative 'that same / the very'. طوق کیخسرو is a possessive construction 'the necklace of Kay Khosrow' (noun followed by possessor). و connects the coordinated item گوشوار 'earring / ornament'. The phrase lists treasure items with the demonstrative applying to them.
segments.s242-g296: همان again 'the same / that very' modifies the following noun یاره (here 'yāreh' used as a named object/attendant — contextually an item or attendant associated with Giv). گیو is the possessor 'of Giv', and گوهرنگار is a postpositive adjective 'gem‑studded / jewel‑adorned' placed after the noun, a common poetic word order.
segments.s242-g345: بدین is the classical compound of به + این (“to/for this”), placed before the noun to mean “to this”; رزمگاه is the noun “battlefield.” آمدستم is a first-person past (classical) verb form “I came/approached” (literary ending -ـستم). فراز is an adverb/particle “up/on high” placed after the verb to indicate upward/approach sense (“came up to”).
segments.s242-g346: بپیموده is a past participle (بـ- prefix + پیموده) meaning “having traversed/covered,” used adverbially to qualify the subject (I). بسیار is an adverb “very/much,” modifying راه “road/path”; دراز is an adjective “long” modifying راه. Word order here is participle phrase + quantified noun phrase: “having traversed a very long road.”
segments.s242-g347: مگر here functions as an interrogative/optative particle (“perhaps/could it be that”), introducing a hopeful or speculative clause. چهره is “face/appearance”; دخت is “daughter/maiden” and names the relationship; افراسیاب is the proper name (Afrasiab). The phrase is the topical NP “perhaps the face of Afrasiab’s daughter.”
segments.s242-g348: نماید is a 3rd-person present/jussive form used optatively here (“may (someone/something) show/reveal”). مرا is the object “me” (here split in the tokenization as م + را); را is the definite/object marker attached to the pronoun. بخت فرخ is the subject phrase “fortunate fate/destiny” (بخت = fate; فرخ = fortunate). بخواب is a contraction of به خواب “in sleep/in a dream” (used adverbially). The verb appears before its subject (a common classical word order) and expresses a wish: “may fortunate fate show her to me in sleep.”
segments.s242-g349: همی before the verb gives a continuous or emphatic present sense (“I keep seeing / indeed I see”); بینم is 1st-person present “I see.” این دشت آراسته is the direct noun phrase “this plain adorned” (آراسته = past participle/adjective “adorned”). The clause is a present observation: “I see this plain adorned.”
segments.s242-g350: چو is the comparative particle “as/like.” بتخانه is “house of idols/temple of images.” چین here means “fold(s)/furrow(s)” (not the country); پر از is the frozen phrase “full of” (پر = “full (of)”), and خواسته is used as “desired/objects of desire” (noun/adjectival sense). The whole simile reads “like an idol-temple, its folds full of desirables.”
segments.s242-g351: اگر introduces a conditional “if.” نیک رایی is a nominal phrase “good counsel/opinion” (نیک = good; رایی = opinion/decision). کنی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن “to do” = “you do/make” (here “if you decide/advise well”). تاج زر following the condition is the first item offered: تاج = crown and زر = gold (golden crown).
segments.s242-g352: ترا (tokenized as ت + را) is the 2nd-person object “to you” (ت = 2nd-person clitic, را = object marker). بخشم is 1st-person singular present/future-like “I will bestow/give” (classical usage of بخشیدن = give/bestow). و links the list of gifts: گوشوار “earring/ornament” and کمر here means “girdle/belt” (a wearable gift). The clause promises these items on the condition stated before.
segments.s242-g25: همه باده خسروانی is a noun phrase: همه ('all') modifies باده خسروانی ('royal wine'). The phrase به دست functions as a prepositional phrase 'in/by the hand(s) of' and here awaits the agent expressed in the next segment; note there is no explicit verb — Persian poetry often omits the copula and uses a sequence of noun phrases to describe the scene.
segments.s242-g26: همه پهلوانان خسروپرست is the agent of the preceding به دست: همه ('all') + پهلوانان ('champions/heroes'); خسروپرست is an adjective/epithet attached to them meaning 'devotees/adorers of Khosrow'. The whole segment completes the idea 'in the hands of all the Khosrow‑worshipping champions.'
segments.s242-g27: می‌اندر قدح is an archaic/poetic locative: می‌ (wine) + اندر (in/within) yields 'wine in the goblet' (قدح = goblet). چون introduces a simile 'like' and links to عقیق یمن 'Yemeni agate' — a common poetic comparison for the color/appearance of the wine.
segments.s242-g28: به پیش اندرون is a locative phrase 'before/in front of the inner apartments' (به + پیش = 'in front of'; اندرون = 'inner quarters'). The following لاله و نسترن is a simple coordinated noun phrase 'tulip and wild rose' placed after the locative to indicate what lay there; again the verb is omitted in poetry.
segments.s242-g29: پریچهرگان ('fair‑faced maidens') is the subject; پیش خسرو ('before Khosrow') gives their position in front of the king, and به پای is the idiom 'at the feet (of)' indicating humility or proximity. Word order places the location phrases after the subject, as is common in Persian descriptive lines.
segments.s242-g30: سر زلفشان is a possessive construction: سر ('top/head') + زلفشان ('their locks', زلف + possessive suffix -شان). بر is the preposition 'on/upon', and سمن مشک‌سای is a compound describing what their tresses lay upon: سمن ('jasmine' or 'sweet scent') qualified by مشک‌سای ('musk‑scented'), i.e. 'musk‑scented jasmine.'
segments.s242-g31: همه بزمگه 'the whole banquet‑hall' (همه modifies بزمگه) is followed by بوی و رنگ بهار 'the scent and color of spring' — a coordinated noun phrase describing the hall's atmosphere. This is a descriptive nominal clause; like the other lines, the copula is omitted and the sequence paints the scene.
segments.s242-g32: کمر بسته is a participial phrase literally 'having girded the waist' (کمر + بسته) — an idiom meaning 'girded/prepared.' بر پیش سالاربار places this state 'before/in front of the quartermaster' (سالاربار, an official in charge of baggage). The line thus says that (those present) were girded/ready in front of the quartermaster; note the participle functions adjectivally without an explicit finite verb.
segments.s242-g57: سوی شهر ایران is a locative/prepositional phrase 'toward the city of Iran' placed at the beginning; یکی بیشه بود is a typical Persian existential clause 'there was a certain grove' with the indefinite modifier یکی before بیشه and the copula بود (past) at the end.
segments.s242-g58: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that'; ما را uses را to mark the experiencer/possessor ('for us' / 'we had'); بدان بیشه is a prepositional phrase 'to/about that grove' (بدان = به + آن); اندیشه بود is a nominative construction 'there was thought' = 'we thought/kept thinking' with بود as the past copula.
segments.s242-g59: چه مایه is an exclamatory quantifier 'what a quantity/abundance'; بدو (به + او) means 'in/into it' or 'within it' here; اندرون کشتزار is a locative nominal group 'inside were cultivated fields' (اندرون = 'inside', کشتزار = 'planted field').
segments.s242-g60: درخت is the subject 'trees'; برآور is a poetic/archaic verbal form 'brought forth/grew' (here treated as past/gnomic verbal action); همه میوه‌دار is a noun/adjective sequence 'all (of them) fruit-bearing', with همه quantifying and میوه‌دار as the attributive adjective.
segments.s242-g61: چراگاه ما بود is a possessive nominal phrase 'it was our pasture' with the possessor ما after the noun and بود as the copula; و connects the next noun phrase فریاد ما 'our cry' which is left intentionally without a verb here because it is picked up by the following vocative/imperative.
segments.s242-g62: ایا begins a vocative/exclamatory address 'O/ah'; شاه ایران is the addressee 'king of Iran'; بده is the imperative 'give!' and داد here is the noun 'justice' (not a past tense verb), so بده داد ما literally 'give us justice' with ما as the object 'us'.
segments.s242-g63: گراز آمد is a simple past clause 'a boar came'; اکنون is an adverb 'now/at present'; فزون از شمار is a predicative phrase 'more than (can be) counted' or 'exceeding number' describing abundance—word order places the temporal/adverbial element and the quantitative comment after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s242-g64: گرفت is a past transitive verb 'seized/took'; آن همه 'all those' is a determiner phrase (آن 'that' + همه 'all'); بیشه و مرغزار are the coordinated objects 'groves and meadows'. The subject (the boar(s)) is omitted here because it was named in the previous clause.
segments.s242-g177: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before its noun. دندان (dandan) is the noun 'tooth/teeth' and here is the source of the action. همی (hamī) is the classical/perfective-continuous particle that gives an iterative or ongoing sense to the following verb. آتش (ātaš) is the direct object 'fire' and افروختند (afroxhtand) is the 3rd-person plural past 'they kindled' — verb comes after object in this clause, typical of Persian poetic word order.
segments.s242-g178: تو (to) is the 2nd-person singular pronoun 'you'. گفتی (gofti) is a past-tense 2nd-person form 'you said' (classical line-internal past). که (ke) introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. گیتی (gīti) is the subject 'the world'. همی again marks the ongoing/experiential sense before سوختند (sukhtand), which is 3rd-person plural past 'they burned' (here 'was burning' applied to the world).
segments.s242-g179: گرازی (garāzī) is a noun 'a boar' (here the actor). بیامد (biyāmad) is the simple past/past narrative 'came'. چو (ču) introduces a simile 'as/like', and آهرمنا (Āhrimnā) is the name 'Ahriman' — so the clause reads 'a boar came like Ahriman.'
segments.s242-g180: زره (zareh) 'armor' is marked by the object marker را (rā) showing it is the definite direct object. بدرید (bedarid) is the past tense 'they/it tore' (3rd-person past). بر (bar) here is a preposition 'on/upon' (or 'at/against') connecting the following personal name: بیژنا (Bīžnā) — a form of Bijan; taken together, the clause means 'they tore the armor upon Bijan' (i.e. tore Bijan's armor).
segments.s242-g181: چو (ču) introduces a simile 'as/like'. سوهان (suhān) is 'file/rasp' and پولاد (pulād) qualifies it 'steel' (a compound-like phrase 'steel file'). بر (bar) + سنگ (sang) 'on/against stone' and سخت (sakht) modifies سنگ as 'hard' — the whole phrase is a simile comparing two abrasive actions ('like a steel file on hard stone').
segments.s242-g182: همی (hamī) again gives iterative/continuous aspect 'kept / was continually'. سود (sud) here functions idiomatically with دندان (dandan) as the acting element meaning to wear/abrade — syntactically it's the verb-like element governing دندان خود (his own tooth); خود (xod) is the reflexive/possessive 'his own'. بر (bar) + درخت (deraxt) is the prepositional phrase 'against/on the tree'. Overall the clause means 'he kept grinding/wearing his own tooth on the tree.'
segments.s242-g183: برانگیختند (barangikhtand) is a past plural verb 'they aroused/raised/sprang up' applied to آتش کارزار (ātash-e kārzār) — 'the fire of the battle' (آتش 'fire' modified by کارزار 'battle'). Word order places the verb before its object for poetic emphasis but the verb is 3rd-person past plural.
segments.s242-g184: برآمد (barāmad) is a past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'. یکی (yeki) here means 'a certain/one' (indefinite) modifying دود (dūd) 'smoke'. زان (zān) is a fused prepositional form 'from that' (از + آن) pointing back to a previously mentioned place; مرغزار (morghzār) is 'meadow'. The clause thus reads 'a smoke arose from that meadow.'
segments.s242-g273: 'برفتند' is the past 3rd‑person plural verb 'they went/left' and is placed before its subject (verbal-first word order, common in poetry). 'هر دو' (each/both) identifies the subject 'both of them'. 'به راه دراز' is a prepositional phrase of direction ('to/on the long road'), where 'به' introduces the goal and 'دراز' is an adjective after the noun 'راه'.
segments.s242-g274: This is a twofold noun phrase describing the two travellers. 'یکی' = 'one (of them)'. 'آز پیشه' is a noun+noun collocation functioning like an adjectival description: 'آز' (avarice/greed) as 'پیشه' (profession/practice) — 'one whose practice is greed'. 'دگر' = 'the other', and 'کینه‌ساز' is an agent noun ('maker/producer of spite/hatred') describing the second. Note the absence of a copula; these are nominal predicates/appositions characterizing the two.
segments.s242-g275: 'میان دو بیشه' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'between two groves' ('میان' + cardinal + plural noun). 'به یک روزه راه' is an adverbial phrase of extent/duration: 'به یک روزه' ('one‑day' as a compound adjective) modifies 'راه' ('road/journey') and together it means 'about a day's journey' or 'a one‑day road'. The adjective‑like '-ه' on 'روزه' is an archaic/poetic form forming 'one‑day'.
segments.s242-g276: 'فرود آمد' is the compound verb 'descended / alighted' (past 3rd singular). The subject follows the verb (verb‑first): 'آن گرد لشکر پناه'. 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that' modifying 'گرد'. 'لشکر پناه' is a compound in which 'پناه' qualifies the army ('a sheltering/protective host' or 'army that provides refuge'); the phrase as a whole denotes the troop/host that descended. (In epic poetry it is common for the verb to precede its noun phrase subject.)
segments.s242-g277: Here the first token is the older/poetic sequence written as two pieces: 'ب' (a particle/prefix) plus 'دان' together function as 'بدان' = 'in/those' (locative/demonstrative). 'مرغزاران' is the plural 'meadows'. 'ارمان دو روز' places 'ارمان' ('desire/pleasure/longing') together with the duration 'دو روز' ('two days') to indicate they spent two days in that state/pleasure — idiomatically 'for two delightful days'.
segments.s242-g278: 'همی' is a continuous/emphatic particle often used before verbs/adjectives in classical Persian to mark ongoing or habitual aspect ('ever/continually'). 'شاد بودند' is the predicate 'they were happy/merry' (past plural). 'با باز و یوز' is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with falcon and cheetah' — 'باز' here is the hunting‑hawk (falcon) sense and 'یوز' is the hunting cheetah; together they indicate hunting/pleasure activities accompanying their merriment.
segments.s242-g279: 'چو' here = 'when'. 'دانست' is the past 3rd‑person singular 'he learned/realized'. 'گرگین' is the personal name Gorgin (proper name). The clause 'که آمد عروس' is a subordinate content clause introduced by 'که' ('that') giving what he learned — 'that the bride came'. The word order places the reporting verb before the subordinate clause, which is typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s242-g280: 'همه دشت' is the subject 'the whole plain'. 'ازو شد' is a causative/ablative turn: 'ازو' = 'because of her / from her', and 'شد' = 'became' (past). 'چو' before a noun phrase is the comparative 'like/as', so 'چو چشم خروس' = 'like the eye of a rooster' — a poetic simile describing the change the plain underwent on her account. Word order is subject → ablative phrase → verb → simile complement.
segments.s242-g337: A compact nominal phrase stating lineage. سیاوش is the topic/name. نیم ('half') modifies the verbal notion نز here used poetically as ‘descended (from)’, so نیم نز پری زادگان = “half-descended from the fairy-born”; پری زادگان is a compound noun ‘fairy‑born/offspring of the fairies.’ Word order is compressed and poetic (modifier before head).
segments.s242-g338: Two linked prepositional/genitive phrases giving the speaker’s origin. از ایرانم = ‘I am of Iran’ (ایران + enclitic ـم functions as predicate/possessive in poetry). The second از introduces تخم آزادگان ‘the seed/stock of the free’ (تخم = lineage/stock, آزادگان = the free/noble), repeating از for emphasis of descent.
segments.s242-g339: An equational introductory clause. منم = ‘I am’ (copular, first‑person), followed by the personal name بیژن and the patronymic گیو (Bijan, Giv). ز ایران = ‘from Iran’ (ز = from, poetic variant of از) and به جنگ = ‘to the battle/for battle’ (به + noun giving direction/purpose).
segments.s242-g340: This clause gives circumstance/result. به زخم گراز آمدم literally ‘I came with/to a boar‑wound’; it is idiomatically read as ‘I came wounded by a boar’ (زخم = wound, گراز = wild boar). آمدم is simple past first‑person ‘I came/arrived,’ and بی‌درنگ is an adverb ‘without delay / immediately’ modifying the action.
segments.s242-g341: Two coordinated verbal actions with the same object implied. سرانشان = ‘their leaders/heads’ (object with possessive ـشان). بریدم = ‘I cut off’ (past‑tense, first person). فگندم is a poetic past form ‘I cast/threw’ (the prefix ف‑ is emphatic/poetic here) and براه = ‘on/onto the road’ (destination/placement).
segments.s242-g342: Purpose/result clause introduced by که (‘that/so that’). دندانهاشان = ‘their teeth/tusks’ (possessed noun). برم is here the past action ‘I carried/took’ (برد in past), with نزد = ‘before / in the presence of’ and شاه = ‘the king’; together: ‘I brought their tusks before the king.’
segments.s242-g343: Temporal clause. چو = ‘when’. زین here functions as a demonstrative ‘that/this’ (archaic poetic form) modifying جشنگاه ‘place/occasion of celebration’ (a compound meaning a festive or ceremonial scene). آگهی یافتم = ‘I became aware / I received news’ (آگهی = information/news, یافتم = ‘I found/learned’ in past tense).
segments.s242-g344: Main clause of motion. سوی = ‘toward(s) / to’, followed by the two names گیو و گودرز (Giv and Goudarz) as the persons toward whom the speaker hastened. نشتافتم is the past first‑person of شتافتن ‘to hasten / rush’; the verb governs the directional سوی phrase.
segments.s242-g249: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking source; عنبرش is عنبر (amber/ambergris) with the enclitic -ش 'its', so 'from its amber'. خاک here means 'earth/soil' (the ground itself) and the phrase follows Persian order Source + Thing (literally 'from its amber, the earth'). The second half repeats the same pattern with ز + یاقوت + سنگ ('from the ruby, a gem/stone').
segments.s242-g250: هوا 'the air' is the subject, followed by مشکبوی, an adjectival compound meaning 'musk‑scented' (noun + بوی). و links the clauses. زمین 'the earth' is then described by the reduplicated رنگ رنگ, where repetition is a poetic intensifier meaning 'many‑colored' or 'variegated' (color + color).
segments.s242-g251: خم‌آورده is a compound perfect participle meaning 'bent' or 'bowed' (lit. 'has made to bend'), governing the rest of the clause. از بار 'from the load/weight of' is a standard prepositional phrase; شاخ 'branch' names what is bowed and سمن 'jasmine' gives the burden (i.e. 'the branch bowed from the weight of jasmine').
segments.s242-g252: صنم here uses the poetic sense 'beloved' rather than literal 'idol'. گشته is the perfect participle/auxiliary 'has become' (turned into). پالیز 'orchard/cultivated field' is the resulting metaphorical state. The clause continues with و + گلبن 'rosebud' + شمن 'sorcerer/charmer', again linking images with گشته understood: 'the beloved became an orchard, and the rosebud (became) a sorcerer.'
segments.s242-g253: خرامان is an adverb meaning 'with a proud/stately gait'. به گرد گل means 'around/about the flowers' (به + گرد = 'around'); اندر is the archaic preposition 'in/among', and تذرو is a verbal stem 'to scatter/disperse' used here as an action associated with the preceding adverbial phrase ('strutting around the flowers, scattering among them').
segments.s242-g254: خروشیدن is an infinitive/verbal noun meaning 'to cry out; to sound forth' (here applied to a bird's loud song). بلبل 'the nightingale' is the agent; از شاخ سرو is a prepositional phrase 'from the bough of the cypress' (شاخ = branch/bough, سرو = cypress). The word order places the action after the earlier descriptive clause for rhythmic effect.
segments.s242-g255: ازین پس کنون is the idiom 'from now on' (از + این/ین + پس). تا introduces a duration 'until/for (a time)', بس here has the sense of 'enough/considerable' and combines with روزگار 'time/age' to indicate a substantial span ('for many days/times'). The sequence places temporal framing before the result clause.
segments.s242-g256: شود is the present/subjunctive of شدن 'become' (here stating the eventual state). چون is the simile 'like/as', followed by بهشت 'paradise/garden'. آن همه 'that whole' (آن = that, همه = all/entire) modifies مرغزار 'meadow', so the clause reads '(may) that entire meadow become like paradise' (in context presented as a statement of how the place will appear).
segments.s242-g73: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' placed before the past-tense verb بشنید ('he heard'), so the clause reads 'when he heard ...'. گفتار فریادخواه is a noun phrase 'the speech/words of the one crying for help' (گفتار = 'speech', فریادخواه = 'one who cries for help' or petitioner) with the modifier following the head.
segments.s242-g74: به درد دل اندر بپیچید is an idiomatic descriptive clause: به ... اندر marks being 'into' a state ('into pain of heart'), and بپیچید is a past-tense intransitive verb 'he twisted/was twisted' conveying being seized by grief. شاه appears after the verb (poetic word order) as the subject: 'the king was twisted into the pain of his heart' = 'the king was overcome with sorrow.'
segments.s242-g75: بریشان is the contracted form بر ایشان ('to/for them'), indicating the indirect object. ببخشود is the simple past transitive 'he forgave/granted'. خسرو follows the verb (poetic order) as the subject: 'Khosrow forgave them (that) pain.' The following به درد qualifies what was forgiven/what they were spared.
segments.s242-g76: به گردان گردنکش is a prepositional phrase 'to the troop, the headstrong/rebellious (one)' where گردان = 'troop' and گردنکش = 'headstrong/rebellious'. آواز کرد is a light-verb construction meaning 'uttered a call / called out / summoned' (آواز = call, کرد = did/made), with the implied subject 'he' (Khosrow) omitted.
segments.s242-g77: که‌ای here functions as an archaic/vocative address ('O' / 'you who are'), followed by the addressees نامداران ('the renowned ones') and گردان من ('my troop' — the possessor من follows the noun in older/poetic Persian). The phrase addresses the group directly.
segments.s242-g78: که at the start of this clause is the relative/interrogative 'who'. جوید is the present/habitual form 'seeks' (the classical present marked by همی), همی is the archaic present/habitual marker here ('still/always' or ongoing), and نام here means 'name' in the sense of reputation/fame. از این انجمن is the prepositional phrase 'from this assembly' (ین is the orthographic form of این). The clause asks who among you seeks fame from this gathering.
segments.s242-g79: شود is an optative/impersonal form often rendered 'may it be / let it be that' (here expressing a wish or curse). سوی این بیشه = 'toward this grove' (سوی = toward, این بیشه = this thicket/grove). خوک خورد is a verbal clause 'pigs eat' (خوک = pig(s), خورد = ate/eat); together the phrase expresses the ill wish 'may he be eaten by swine' or 'may pigs eat him', with the event treated as something that happens.
segments.s242-g80: به نام بزرگ و به ننگ و نبرد strings together prepositional phrases introduced by به ('with/regarding' or 'bearing'): 'with the great name and with shame and battle.' نام بزرگ = 'great name' (fame), ننگ = 'shame', نبرد = 'battle/strife' — these are listed as the consequences or accompaniments of the fate just invoked.
segments.s242-g185: بزد is a past action 'he struck' (archaic/poetic past of زدن) and takes the instrument and target that follow. خنجری is the instrument 'a dagger' (indefinite). بر میان بیژنش is a prepositional phrase 'into/against the middle of Bijan' where بر + میان = 'at/in the middle of' and the -ش clitic on بیژن marks possession ('Bijan's').
segments.s242-g186: بدو is the dative/clitic meaning 'to him' (i.e. referring back to Bijan). نیمه شد is a passive/resultative phrase 'was made half / became halved' (شد used as past 'became / was'). پیل پیکر تنش is a compound noun phrase 'his elephant-bodied body' (pīl-pīkar = 'elephant-sized/huge-bodied', تن + ـش = 'his body'), serving as the subject/patient of 'نیمه شد'.
segments.s242-g187: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. روبه شدند is an older/poetic verbal phrase 'turned toward / charged' (روبه here = 'toward' + شدند 'they became/turned'), and آن ددان دلیر is the noun phrase 'those brave beasts/foes' (آن = 'those/that', دلیر = 'brave').
segments.s242-g188: تن از تیغ پر خون is a verbless clauseal description: 'their bodies, from the sword, full of blood'—پر follows a noun to mean 'full (of)'. دل از جنگ سیر is parallel: 'their hearts, from battle, sated' (سیر used adjectivally = 'sated/satisfied'). The repeated از marks source/cause ('from the sword', 'from the war').
segments.s242-g189: سرانشان is 'their leaders' or 'their heads' (سران = heads/leaders, -شان = their) and appears before the verb for emphasis. به خنجر ببرید is an instrumental/verb phrase 'cut off with a dagger'—ببرید here is the past action 'they cut' (classical past of بریدن). پست is an adjective 'low/base' describing the manner/condition or the men removed.
segments.s242-g190: به فتراک شبرنگ سرکش ببست: به + noun + verb is an idiomatic frame 'tied/bound to (the) ...'. فتراک (saddle-cloth / cloak) is the item, modified by شبرنگ 'variegated/spotty'; سرکش is an adjective ('rebellious/proud') applied to the tied heads/objects. ببست is the past 'he/they bound/tied' (here 'they tied them to the cloth').
segments.s242-g191: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'so/that/which'. دندانها نزد شاه آورد is 'they brought the tusks before the king'—نزد = 'in the presence of / before', and آورد is the past 'brought'. Here دندانها (literally 'teeth' or 'tusks') functions as the direct object that was presented to the king.
segments.s242-g192: تن بی‌سرانشان به راه آورد is a compact clause 'they brought/led their headless bodies on the road'—تن بی‌سرانشان = 'their headless bodies' (بی‌سر = headless, with possessive -شان), and به راه آورد is an idiom meaning 'led along the road / paraded (them)'.
segments.s242-g193: به گردان ایران نماید هنر is a prepositional phrase + verbal clause: به گردان ایران (to/for the host of Iran) is the indirect object/scene, followed by نماید (third‑person singular, narrative/poetic present/past) 'shows' or 'displayed', and then هنر (skill/valor) as the thing shown. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: the verb precedes the direct object (هنر).
segments.s242-g194: ز پیلان جنگی جدا کرده سر is a participial/ablative clause that gives the manner/result: ز + پیلان جنگی = 'from the war‑elephants'; جدا کرده is a perfective participle 'having separated / having cut off'; سر (here final) is the direct object 'head(s)'. The subject is omitted and is the same as the verb in the previous segment (he). The sequence places the source (ز پیلان جنگی) before the participle and ends with the thing acted on (سر), a common poetic order.
segments.s242-g195: به گردون برافگند is a verbal clause: به گردون 'into the sky/heaven' + برافگند (past/poetic 3rd‑sg) 'threw up' or 'flung'. هر (each) + (implicit یک) + چو کوه 'as/like a mountain' is a simile modifying each thrown object. The phrase presents the action vividly: each item was flung up, each one like a mountain.
segments.s242-g196: بشد گاومیش از کشیدن ستوه has بَشد (an older narrative past 'it became/was made') as the verb and گاومیش (subject) 'the water‑buffalo'; از + کشیدن is an ablative construction 'from (the) pulling/hauling' where کشیدن is the verbal noun/infinitive, and ستوه is a predicate adjective 'weary, fed up'. So: 'the buffalo became weary from pulling.'
segments.s242-g197: بداندیش گرگین شوریده رفت stacks epithets before the verb: بداندیش 'ill‑minded/evil‑hearted' modifies the proper name گرگین (here the character Gorgin), شوریده 'distraught / raving' is another adjective, and رفت is the simple past 'he went' or 'left'. Grammatically the line uses several modifiers in series before the finite verb, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s242-g198: ز یک سوی بیشه درآمد چو تفت is a locative + verbal clause: ز یک سوی بیشه 'from one side of the thicket' (ز + noun indicating source/location), درآمد here functions as the verbal 'he entered / appeared / came forth' (noun sense 'entrance' in the provided senses corresponds to this verbal use), and چو تفت is a simile 'like a scorch/flash' (تفت used metaphorically). The whole means he emerged from the thicket suddenly, like a scorching blast.
segments.s242-g199: همه بیشه آمد به چشمش کبود: همه بیشه 'the whole grove/thicket' is the subject, آمد is the verb 'came/appeared', and به چشمش کبود is a predicative construction where به چشمش 'to his eye' marks the experiencer and کبود is the adjective 'dark/dusky' describing how it appeared to him. Word order places the experiencer phrase before the adjective, typical in Persian.
segments.s242-g200: برو آفرین کرد و شادی نمود contains an interjective phrase and two verbs: برو آفرین is an idiomatic exclamation translated 'Bravo!' (برو here functions within the set phrase rather than a literal imperative), کرد 'he made/uttered' takes آفرین as its object (i.e. 'he cried “bravo”'), و شادی نمود 'and showed/displayed joy' uses نمود (poetic past of نشان دادن) to mean 'he manifested or expressed joy.'
segments.s242-g281: This clause is literal S(ubject omitted)-O-V order typical of classical Persian: the indirect object/prepositional phrase 'به بیژن' ('to Bijan') comes first, then the temporal/sequence adverb 'پس' ('then'), the demonstrative noun phrase 'آن داستان' ('that story') as the direct object, and finally the past-tense verb 'برگشاد' ('opened'). 'برگشاد' is an older/poetic perfect/past form (بر‑ + گشاد) meaning 'opened'. The actual subject (he) is omitted and recovered from context.
segments.s242-g282: 'وزان' here is a connective/adverbial 'and/from that' linking this clause to the previous one; it introduces what follows as derived from that tale. The idiom 'یاد کردن' (here split as noun + auxiliary 'یاد' + 'کرد') means 'to remember' — 'جشن و رامش' are the things remembered, 'بسی' is an adverb modifying the verb (much/many), and 'کرد' is the past-tense auxiliary 'did/made', so the literal order is '[from that] he made/caused remembrance of many feasts and comforts' = 'he remembered many feasts and pleasures.'
segments.s242-g283: Word order here is somewhat flexible: 'به گرگین' ('to Gorgin') is the indirect object, 'چنین گفت' ('said thus') is the verb phrase, and 'پس بیژنا' places the subject/name ('then Bijan') after the verb for emphasis — a not-uncommon poetic order. Note that the listed sense entries for 'گفت' did not include the verb 'said', so that item is marked accordingly.
segments.s242-g284: The clause introduced by 'که' is a reported content clause: 'that I ...'. 'من' is the explicit first-person subject, 'پیشتر' ('earlier/first') modifies the verb, and 'سازم' is the present/subjunctive form of 'ساختن/سازیدن' meaning 'I will arrange/prepare' or 'I shall make ready.' 'این رفتنا' is the object: 'this going/departure' (poetic nominalized infinitive). Together: 'that I will arrange this departure sooner/beforehand.'
segments.s242-g285: Here 'شوم' is a subjunctive/optative first-person form of 'شدن' used as a modal auxiliary ('I will go / let me go / I shall be so that I...'); it precedes the object 'بزمگه را' (marked by 'را') and the verb 'ببینم' ('that I may see' — present/subjunctive). 'ز دور' is an adverbial phrase 'from afar'. The construction expresses intent/purpose: 'I will go to the banquet-hall in order to behold it from afar.'
segments.s242-g286: 'که' introduces the explanatory/reason clause ('for/because'). 'ترکان' is the subject ('the Turks'), 'همی' is an older continuous/iterative particle meaning roughly 'are/indeed' in narrative style, and the predicate is nominal/elliptical: 'چون بسیچند سور' = 'so many feasts.' Persian poetry commonly omits a copular verb here; the sense is 'for the Turks are indeed making so many feasts.'
segments.s242-g287: 'وز' here is a poetic connective (akin to 'و از' = 'and from') linking the action to the previous location; 'آن جایگه' is 'that place'. 'پس' is temporal/adverbial 'then', and 'بتابم' is the present/subjunctive form (ب‑ + تافت/تاب) meaning 'I turn' or 'I bend/guide' (i.e., I turn the reins). 'عنان' is the direct object 'the reins'. The verb is first-person and modal, expressing intended action.
segments.s242-g288: This final phrase gives the manner/goal: 'به گردن برآرم' — 'I lift/put up to the neck' where 'به گردن' marks the target and 'برآرم' is the first-person present/subjunctive of 'برآوردن'/'برآرم' meaning 'to raise/lift up'. 'ز' is the ablative 'from/of' and links the following compound 'دوده سنان' — 'دوده' ('smoke/soot') modifying 'سنان' ('spear'), a compact poetic genitive/compound. The clause is somewhat elliptical and idiomatic in poetry: 'I raise it to the neck from the spear's soot' (i.e. a smoky or sooty spear).
segments.s242-g329: بدین بزمگه بر ندیدیم کس is a compact locative + negated-past clause. بدین here is a frozen locative phrase 'به + این' = 'to/at this' (rendered as 'in this/at this'); بزمگه 'banquet-place/assembly' is the locative noun. بر is a prepositional/locative particle here ('upon/at/to') that links the clause to the place. ندیدیم is the past tense, 1st-person plural negative of دیدن ('we did not see'); کس is the indefinite 'anyone/someone' and is the object of ندیدیم (negation makes it 'no one/anyone ... not').
segments.s242-g330: ترا دیدم ای سرو آزاده بس is the speaker's counterstatement. ترا is the 2nd-person object (here split into ت + را) meaning 'you'; دیدم is past 1st-singular 'I saw'. ای is the vocative 'O', introducing the address; سرو is a metaphorical epithet 'cypress' (= tall/beautiful person) and آزاده is an adjective 'noble/proud/free' modifying it. بس is a final exclamation 'enough' / 'that suffices' emphasizing that seeing this one outranks all others.
segments.s242-g331: چو دایه بر بیژن آمد فراز is a temporal clause: چو = 'when'; دایه is the subject 'the nurse'. بر + بیژن = 'to/toward Bijan' (classical locative/benefactive use). آمد is the past tense 'came'. فراز here is adverbial 'up/forward, aloud' (used poetically to indicate rising voice or moving forward) and attaches to آمد to specify how/where she came.
segments.s242-g332: برو آفرین کرد و بردش نماز is a coordinated sequence of actions. آفرین (an exclamation 'bravo' / 'praise') is the direct object of کرد ('she made/uttered'), so آفرین کرد = 'she uttered praise/said "bravo"'. و = 'and'. بردش is the past form of بردن with the object clitic (-ش) meaning 'she took/carried him' (to/for something); نماز following names the goal/purpose 'to (perform) prayer'. The initial برو here is an interjection/verb 'go' in imperative form in isolation in the text (its exact pragmatic force is idiomatic/poetic); the main grammatical chain is آفرین کرد و بردش نماز.
segments.s242-g333: پیام منیژه به بیژن بگفت is a straightforward SOV clause in past tense: پیام 'message' is the theme, منیژه is the possessor/agent reference, به بیژن = 'to Bijan' (indirect object), and بگفت is the past narrative form 'she told/said' (بگفت = گفت with the ezafe/old prefix), so 'she told Bijan the message / Manijeh's message she told to Bijan.'
segments.s242-g334: همه روی بیژن چو گل بر شکفت is a simile describing Bijan's reaction. جمله order is topicalized: همه روی بیژن = 'Bijan's entire face' (همه = whole/entire + روی = face), چو = 'as/like', گل = 'a flower', بر شکفت = 'bloomed' (شکفت past 'blossomed'; بر here is an emphatic prepositional particle 'upon/over' used with شکفت). The meaning: 'Bijan's whole face bloomed like a flower.'
segments.s242-g335: چنین پاسخ آورد بیژن بدوی is a reporting clause. چنین پاسخ آورد = 'he brought/gave such an answer' (آورد used as 'gave/uttered'), بیژن is the subject 'Bijan'. بدوی is an adjectival qualifier meaning 'primitive/first/simple' (here qualifying the reply as simple/original). Word order is typical classical Persian: adverb/phrase + verb + subject + modifier.
segments.s242-g336: که من‌ای فرستاده خوب روی begins the content of Bijan's reply introduced by که = 'that'. من‌ای is vocative/quoted-first-person 'I — O...', where من = 'I' and the attached ای is the vocative particle addressing the messenger. فرستاده = 'messenger' (the addressee), خوب here modifies روی to give خوب‌روی 'fair-faced' / 'beautiful-faced' as a compound epithet, and روی = 'face'. Grammatically this is the opening of a direct speech clause: 'that I — O fair-faced messenger — ...'.
segments.s242-g409: نهفته is a participial/adjectival qualifier ('hidden, concealed') placed before the verbal clause to describe the subject. The phrase به کاخ اندر آمد is a classical word order for motion: به + noun (کاخ) marks direction/toward, اندر is the older preposition 'in/within' that accompanies the verb آمد (past 'came'), and به شب is an adverbial use of به + شب ‘by/at night’ marking time. The overall construction means '(he) came into the palace at night, concealed.'
segments.s242-g410: به بیگانگان is a prepositional phrase 'to/for strangers' placed before the verb. هیچ here is an emphatic negative ('not at all/never') modifying the verbal phrase. نگشاد is a negative past form (ن‑ + گشاد(ن) ~ 'did not open') governing لب ('lip'); the literal order 'هیچ نگشاد لب' corresponds to 'he did not open his lip(s) at all.'
segments.s242-g411: چو here is the classical conjunction 'when'. بیدار شد is a periphrastic past ('became awake' = 'woke up'), with شد as the auxiliary/past marker. The coordinated و connects the next result clause هوش یافت ('found his senses' = 'regained consciousness'); یافت is the past of یابـدن used here as 'to recover/find (one's) awareness.'
segments.s242-g412: نگار سمن‌بر is a noun phrase (name/epithet: 'Negar Samanbar'—the beloved, épithète). در آغوش is a locative phrase 'in [his] embrace', and یافت again is the past 'he found'—object + locative precede the verb in this poetic ordering, so the clause means 'he found Negar Samanbar in (his) arms.'
segments.s242-g413: به ایوان افراسیاب is a locative prepositional phrase 'at/to the iwan of Afrasiab' (به marking location/direction). اندرا following it is the proper name 'Andra' (the agent/subject) placed after the locative phrase—classical Persian permits such inversion for emphasis or meter.
segments.s242-g414: ابا here functions as a verb/adverbial notion 'with reluctance / was reluctant' modifying the subsequent action. ماه‌رخ ('moon‑faced') qualifies the person whose سر ('head') is governed by the motion phrase سر به بالین برا: 'laid/placed the head toward/upon the pillow.' برا is an older directional/resultive particle meaning 'to/toward' or 'for', completing the motion 'place head on the pillow.'
segments.s242-g415: بپیچید is a past‑tense verb 'he turned/twisted' (3rd sg.), here reflexive in sense because it takes بر خویشتن 'around himself' (بر + خویشتن). بیژنا is a name or vocative identifying the actor (an archaic or dialectal form of Bijan) placed after the verb in the classical order.
segments.s242-g416: به یزدان بنالید is 'he wailed/lamented to God' (بنالید = past of نالیدن, with the verbal prefix ب‑). ز introduces the cause/agent of complaint: ز آهرمنا 'because of / on account of Ahriman' (Ahriman = the Evil One). The sequence gives 'he cried to God, (lamenting) because of Ahriman.'
segments.s242-g361: فرستاد is a simple past (3rd sing.) 'he sent'. پاسخ is the direct object 'an answer/reply'. هم here is an emphatic/added particle ('also' / 'indeed') that stresses immediacy; اندر زمان is a prepositional phrase 'at that moment' with اندر + زمان (time). Word order is verb–object–adverbial, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s242-g362: کت is used here in the sense of 'book/letter' (a rare sense). آمد is the past 'arrived'. بدست functions as a locative phrase 'into the hand' (not the instrumental 'by'). آنچ is a relative/pronominal adverb 'that which / the one that', and بردی is perfect/past 'had taken / you had taken' (used to indicate who was thought to possess it). گمان is a noun 'conjecture/expectation' linked to آنچ (the one you thought had it).
segments.s242-g363: گر is the conditional 'if'. آیی is present/subjunctive second person 'you come' (classical Persian often uses present-subjunctive after گر). خرامان is an adverb/adjectival adverb meaning 'with a stately/proud gait'. به نزدیک من places the motion/direction 'toward close to me' (به + نزدیک + من). Word order: conditional clause starting with گر + verb.
segments.s242-g364: بیفروزی is a present-future-stem form meaning 'you will cause to shine / you will illuminate' (2nd person implied by ending). این جان تاریک من is the object phrase 'this dark soul/my dark life' (این = 'this', جان = 'soul/life', تاریک = adjectival 'dark', من = possessive 'my' in context). The verb expresses resultive/optative sense — 'you would brighten'.
segments.s242-g365: نماند is past 'there remained not / was no longer left'. آنگهی 'thereupon/then' time adverb modifies the clause. جایگاه سخن is a nominal phrase 'a place/room for speech' (جایگاه = place, سخن = speech). The negative verb plus noun phrase yields 'no place for words remained.'
segments.s242-g366: خرامید is past 'he/it strode proudly' (third-person past). زان (= ز + آن) is a prepositional/relational form 'from that'. سایه is 'shade' and سروبن is an epithet 'cypress-stem' (a cypress-like person/tree); the verb precedes the prepositional phrase, a common poetic order: 'strode from that shade the cypress.'
segments.s242-g367: سوی is a directional preposition 'toward'. خیمه is the goal 'the tent'. دخت آزاده خوی is a noun phrase 'the maiden (دخت) of noble-hearted disposition' where آزاده qualifies the maiden and خوی further specifies her disposition (adjectival use). The whole phrase is the object of سوی.
segments.s242-g368: پیاده indicates manner 'on foot'. همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle often used in epic verse to indicate ongoing action (here with a past verb). گام (step) + زد (lit. 'struck') form the idiom 'took a step / walked' (زد used idiomatically). با آرزوی is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'with longing/wish' modifying the action 'stepped'.
segments.s242-g49: کجا is an interrogative/adverb ('where' / 'in what place') placed at the start for rhetorical effect. خان is the noun ‘khan’ (lord) and ارمانش is ارمان + enclitic -ش ‘his/its’ ('his desire'); خوانند is the classical 3rd‑person plural present form used here as 'they call' (an archaic/poetic present), and نام is the direct object 'name'. The clause literally reads 'Where they call the khan his desire (by) name' — i.e. 'where the khan is called by that name of desire.'
segments.s242-g50: This clause uses poetic ellipsis: there is no overt verb like آمد (came), so the sense is 'and/from the Armanians a message (was) to Khosrow'. وز here functions as a connective/archaic 'and/from' linking clauses (not one of the provided dictionary senses, so marked separately). ارمانیان is the plural agent ('the Armanians'), نزد + خسرو means 'in the presence of / to Khosrow', and پیام is the object 'message'.
segments.s242-g51: که introduces a wish/relative clause ('that' / 'so that') describing a hoped‑for state. نوشه is a noun 'draught' (a sip/nectar), زی‌ای is an archaic/vocative imperative form of زِی/زی 'live' (زی + ای addressing the king), and شاه is the vocative 'O king'. تا + جاودان is a duration expression 'until/for ever' ('تا' = 'until', جاودان = 'eternal'). The whole clause is an optative blessing: 'May the king live by that draught forever.'
segments.s242-g52: This clause continues the wish: به هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase 'over/for every country/realm'. دسترس (literally 'access' or 'reach') with بر expresses dominion or influence 'reach over'.
