The final token is written in two pieces (ب + دان) in the word list, but in context the line voices an optative 'may there be (to him) / may he have' — the verb is elliptical/poetic. Grammatically, the phrase functions as a benediction: 'may (he) have reach over every land.'
segments.s242-g53: به هر هفت کشور is a prepositional phrase 'over each/ all the seven countries' (classical hyperbolic phrase for the known world). تویی is the emphatic poetic second‑person present 'thou art' (predicate clitic), and شهریار is the predicate noun 'sovereign/king'. Word order is marked (prepositional phrase + pronoun + predicate noun).
segments.s242-g54: ز هر بد is an ablative phrase 'from / against every ill/evil'. تو باشی is a subjunctive/optative second‑person form ('may you be' or 'be thou'), with تو as subject and باشی (classical subjunctive) expressing a wish. بهر شهر is 'for the city' (بهر = for / to the benefit of), and یار is a noun meaning 'companion/ally/protector'. The idiom reads as a supplication: 'From every harm may you be (to) the city a companion/protector.'
segments.s242-g55: سر مرز (literally 'head/top of the border') is an idiom meaning 'at the frontier/border of'; توران names the land 'Turan'. در شهر ماست uses در + شهر 'in the city' with ماست (ما + ست) the copula 'is ours / is our' (inverted order: 'in the city is ours' = 'our city is located there'). The clause therefore states location: 'At Turan's border is our city.'
segments.s242-g56: ازیشان (از + ایشان) is 'from them / because of them'; به ما is 'to us'; بر is the preposition 'upon/against'; چه مایه = an interrogative phrase 'what sort/what cause' (چه = what, مایه = cause/substance); and بلاست is a nominal predicate meaning 'is calamity/affliction' (here used interrogatively). The clause asks rhetorically 'Because of them, to us, what cause of calamity is there?' — i.e. 'what trouble do they bring upon us?'
segments.s242-g145: ز + noun phrase marks source or agent: ز چنگال یوزان = "from/by the claws of the cheetahs." همه دشت = "the whole/plain entire plain" (همه used distributively/collectively). غرم follows and names the state of the plain (a poetic nominal predicate: "wrath/anger"); the copula is omitted, typical of classical Persian poetry, so the clause reads roughly "the whole plain (was) in wrath from the cheetahs' claws." Note the genitive relation is expressed by simple juxtaposition (چنگالِ یوزان).
segments.s242-g146: دریده is a past-participle/adjectival predicate meaning "torn/rent" and links back to دشت (the plain) so the full sense is "the plain was torn." بر here is the spatial/adverbial particle "upon" or an intensifier attached to دِریده to yield "rent open." و connects to the next clause. دل پر از is a standard construction meaning "the heart (was) full of..." (پر + از + noun). داغ and گرم form a coordinated description of emotional state: داغ = "burning grief" and گرم = "hot/fervent"; the copula is omitted, as is common in poetry.
segments.s242-g147: همه گردن = "every/all neck(s)" (همه here distributive: every). گور زخم is a compact noun phrase where گور (grave) qualifies زخم (wound) — "a grave-wound" or "deathly wound." کمند placed after them names the instrument/agent "of the snare/lasso." The clause has no explicit verb (copula omitted): the construction states the condition of all necks as being a grave-wound of the snare.
segments.s242-g148: The pair چه ... چه ... is a correlative used to list alternatives: "whether ... or ..." (classical usage). Here it introduces the two named persons: بیژن and طهمورث; دیوبند is a geographical epithet attached to طهمورث ("Tahmūrath of Diyōband"). The whole phrase is in apposition to the previous clause, specifying that the same fate held for either name.
segments.s242-g149: تذروان is a verbal form meaning "they scattered/they put to flight" (third-person plural sense in this narrative context). به + NP here indicates direction/instrument: به چنگال باز = "by/with the falcons' talons" (چنگال 'talon' + باز 'falcon' in juxtaposition = 'the falcon's talon'). اندرون is the destination/locative "inward/into the inner parts." Note that classical poetry often drops the ezāfe linking the two nouns.
segments.s242-g150: چکان is a verb (or participial verbal form) meaning "it dripped/was dripping." از هوا = "from the air/from above" gives the source; بر = "upon/on" marks the target; سمن برگ is a compound (سمن 'jasmine' + برگ 'leaf') meaning "jasmine-leaves;" خون at the end names what dripped: "blood." Word order places the verb before its patient (poetic inversion); again the copula is absent because the verb is explicit (dripping).
segments.s242-g151: بدین سان is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning "in this manner/thus." همی is a continuous/aspectual particle indicating ongoing action ("kept on/continued"). راه بگذاشتند is a compound verbal expression: راه (way) + بگذاشتند (they set/place/left) = "they set forth on the road / they continued on their way." The verb بگذاشتند is past plural; the prefixed ب- is an archaic/preverbal element preserved in classical prose/poetry.
segments.s242-g152: همه دشت را = "the whole plain" with را marking it as the definite object/topic. باغ پنداشتند is past plural of پنداشتن "to imagine/regard/think": they regarded/took (it) as a garden. The construction is verb-final (typical): object/topic first, predicate after; the copula is fused into پنداشتند ('they thought/regarded').
segments.s242-g353: مرا + را here form the object pronoun 'مرا' (me); the pronoun is placed before the verb for emphasis, with the clitic 'را' marking it as definite/direct object. 'سوی' is a preposition 'toward', 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that', 'خوب‌چهر' is a compound adjective 'fair‑faced', and 'آوری' is the present/jussive form of آوردن used as a request/command ('bring' or 'bring about') with the verb at the end in typical Persian word order.
segments.s242-g354: 'دلش' = دل + ـش (her/ his heart); the possessive enclitic attaches to 'دل'. 'با دل من' literally 'with my heart' (دل من = my heart). 'به مهر آوری' uses 'به' + noun 'مهر' (love/affection) and the same verb form 'آوری' (here 'cause' or 'bring about'); together the clause means 'cause her heart and my heart to be (in) love/affection' — i.e. unite them in love. Word order places the affected noun phrases before the verb, a normal pattern in Persian.
segments.s242-g355: 'چو' here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'بیژن' is the proper name Bijan. 'چنین' = 'thus/so'. 'گفت' is the past form 'said' (here introducing what was said). 'شد' is the verb 'became' (a change/result auxiliary) and 'دایه باز' is an inverted poetic order meaning 'the nurse again' or 'the nurse resumed (speaking)'; together 'شد دایه باز' conveys that the nurse again began to speak or opened (her mouth) again.
segments.s242-g356: 'بگوش' is a prepositional/adverbial compound meaning 'to the ear' (into the ear of). 'منیژه' is the addressee/name Manijeh. 'سرایید' is an older/poetic past form of سرودن/سراییدن meaning 'sang' or 'uttered' (here 'whispered/uttered'). 'راز' is the noun 'secret'. The verb comes last and takes 'بگوش منیژه' as its indirect object: 'she uttered the secret into Manijeh's ear.'
segments.s242-g357: 'که' introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/for'. 'رویش' = 'روی' (face) + enclitic 'ش' ('her'), 'چنینست' is a fused form meaning 'is thus'/'is such'. 'بالا چنین' is a poetically inverted pair: 'بالا' (lofty/high) followed by 'چنین' (thus), an intensifying repetition typical of epic diction — literally 'high, such'. The clause describes her face and ends with the predicate 'چنینست'.
segments.s242-g358: 'چنین' ('thus') functions adverbially. 'آفریدش' is the past simple of آفریدن with the object enclitic 'ش' = 'created her' (Creator created her). 'جهان‌آفرین' is an appositive/title 'the world‑creator' (i.e. God). Word order places the adverb first, verb+enclitic next, and the agent/appositive last: 'Thus the Creator created her.'
segments.s242-g359: 'چو' again = 'when' marking a temporal clause. 'بشنید' is the past tense 'he heard'. 'از دایه او' literally 'from the nurse' (از = from/of, دایه = nurse, او = her/he — here marking agent/source). 'این سخن' = 'this speech/these words'. The clause reads smoothly as 'When he heard this from the nurse...'.
segments.s242-g360: 'بفرمود' is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said' (he instructed). 'رفتن' is the infinitive 'to go' used after a verb of command ('ordered to go'). 'سوی' = 'toward', 'سرو' = 'cypress' (often a lofty tree image), and 'بن' = 'base/foot' (i.e. the root/foot of the tree). Together the clause means 'he ordered (that they) go to the foot of the cypress.'
segments.s242-g81: ببرد is a past third-person singular of برد 'to take/carry' used here as 'he carried off/took'. سران گرازان is a genitive construction 'the heads of the boars' (سران = 'heads' placed before the possessor). به + تیغ marks the instrument ('with the sword'), where به functions as an instrumental preposition.
segments.s242-g82: ندارم is the first-person present (or stative) 'I do not have' used with the noun دریغ to mean 'I have no reluctance / I do not begrudge'. ازو ('from him/of him') specifies the person toward whom the speaker has no reluctance, and گنج گوهر is a coordinated noun phrase 'treasure and gems'. The whole clause therefore expresses 'I will not withhold treasure and jewels from him.'
segments.s242-g83: یکی خوان زرین is a noun phrase 'a certain / one golden feast/table' (یکی used here as 'a certain'). بفرمود is the honorific past third-person 'he commanded/ordered' and the subject شاه ('the king') follows the verb, a common order in epic Persian where the verb may precede its subject.
segments.s242-g84: ک is the relative/conjunction 'that/which'. بنهاد is past 'he placed/put'. گنجور here denotes a treasury/treasure-house, and در پیشگاه means 'in the presence of the court' or 'before the throne' (در = 'in', پیشگاه = 'royal presence/ court'). The clause thus says he set up a treasury in the royal presence.
segments.s242-g85: ز هر گونه گوهر is a partitive phrase 'of/from every kind of gem' (ز used poetically for 'of'). برو here is an archaic compound of بر او ('on it/thereon') used adverbially, and ریختند is past third-person plural 'they poured/scattered'. Together: 'they poured gems of every kind upon it.'
segments.s242-g86: همه یک 'all as one' uses یکی/یک as an adverbial-like modifier meaning 'into one / altogether'. بدیگر means 'to one another' or 'with each other' and برآمیختند is past third-person plural 'they mixed/mingled'. The clause states that everything was mixed together into one.
segments.s242-g87: ده اسب is 'ten horses' (numeral before noun). گرانمایه is an adjective meaning 'precious/valuable' modifying اسب, and زرین لگام is an attributive phrase 'golden bridles' (زرین = 'golden' modifying لگام = 'bridle'). The whole noun phrase describes 'ten valuable horses with golden bridles.'
segments.s242-g88: نهاده is past 'they placed/put' (or 'they set'). برو is the archaic contraction of بر او ('on them/thereon') used adverbially to indicate placement on/onto. داغ here means 'brand/mark', and کاوس نام is an appositive/genitive 'the name Kavus' (i.e. the brand bearing the name Kāvus). The clause therefore means they set on them the brand 'Kavus'.
segments.s242-g169: به بیشه درآمد: the preposition به + location (بیشه) precedes the verb; درآمد here functions as a past action 'entered' (poetic/past). "به کردار شیر" is an adverbial phrase introduced by به meaning 'in the manner of' (used adverbially) and کردار 'conduct/behavior' is followed by شیر 'lion' to give the simile 'like a lion.' Word order is typical of Persian poetry: locative phrase + verb, then manner phrase.
segments.s242-g170: کمان را بزه کرد مرد دلیر: کمان را is the definite object marked by را ('the bow'); بزه is the noun/element that combines with the light verb کرد (کرد = 'did/made' in past) to form the verbal idea (the exact archaic meaning of بزه isn't in the provided senses); the subject مرد دلیر follows the verb for poetic inversion and identifies the agent 'the brave man.'
segments.s242-g171: چو ابر بهاران بغرید سخت: چو introduces a temporal/simile clause ('when'/'as'); ابر بهاران is a genitive-like phrase 'spring clouds'; بغرید is past tense 'roared' (3rd singular); سخت is an adverb 'strongly/mightily' modifying بغرید.
segments.s242-g172: فرو ریخت پیکان چو برگ درخت: فرو is an adverbial prefix/adverb 'downward' attached to the verb ریخت (here 'poured/fell'); Persian poetic word order places the verb before its noun (فرو ریخت پیکان = 'arrows poured down'). چو ... درخت is a simile 'like the leaves of a tree.'
segments.s242-g173: برفت از پس خوک چون پیل مست: برفت is a poetic past verb (not matched in the provided noun senses) functioning as 'he went/charged/attacked'; از پس literally 'from after' = 'after/behind' (here 'in pursuit of'); خوک is 'boar' or 'hog'; چون introduces the simile 'like'; پیل مست = 'a drunken elephant'—a figurative intensifier describing the manner of his attack.
segments.s242-g174: یکی خنجر آب‌داده به دست: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (here introducing the item); خنجر is the noun 'dagger'; آب‌داده is a past-participle modifier 'watered/wet' (figuratively 'moistened'—context implies blood-wet); به دست is a locative/instrumental phrase 'in (his) hand' following the noun phrase.
segments.s242-g175: همه جنگ را پیش او تاختند: همه جنگ (the whole fight/array of battle) is a collective subject; را marks the definite object (here used with the collective notion); پیش او 'before/in front of him' indicates direction/target; تاختند is past plural 'they charged/rushed/attacked.' The object-like use of جنگ with همه emphasizes the totality of the assault.
segments.s242-g176: زمین را به دندان برانداختند: زمین را ('the ground' marked by را) is the object; به دندان is an instrumental phrase 'with (their) teeth' using به + noun to indicate means; برانداختند is past plural 'they overturned/they cast up' (here 'tore up' or 'threw up').
segments.s242-g41: This clause is a coordinated verbal phrase. بگفت is a simple past narrative verb 'he said/told' followed by the relative/indirect object آنچ + بشنید 'that which he heard' (an embedded clause in past). و links the two actions, and فرمان گزید is another past clause where فرمان is the object 'the command/order' and گزید is a past tense verb 'he chose/issued' (classical usage: 'to choose' used for issuing an order). Word order is Verb–Object in the first part (بگفت آنچ بشنید) then Object–Verb in the second (فرمان گزید), typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s242-g42: This short clause gives the result/place: به پیش اندر آوردشان is literal 'brought them into/forward before (him)'. به پیش = 'to/in the presence of', اندر آوردشان is a compound perfective 'brought them in/forward' (آورد + object suffix -شان). چون سزید is a temporal/conditional phrase 'when/as it was fitting' with چون = 'as/when' and سزید (from سزایدن) a past form meaning 'it was fitting/was deserved'.
segments.s242-g43: This participial phrase describes posture: به کش کرده دست is a circumstantial participle 'having stretched/pulled out (their) hand(s)' where به + کش کرده forms a verbal-adverbial phrase and دست is the object. و links زمین را به روی — 'the ground onto (their) face' with را marking زمین as definite object and به روی indicating 'on (their) face'. The whole phrase is a descriptive prelude to the main verb in the next segment.
segments.s242-g44: ستردند is a past tense verb meaning 'they covered/hid/veiled' (here the sense is covering the face or drawing a veil over oneself) and functions as the main verb. زاری‌کنان is an adverbial/participle 'lamenting' (the -کنان suffix marks ongoing manner). پیش اوی = 'before him' (پیش = in the presence of; اوی is the archaic/pronounced form of 'him/it'). The clause thus means they, lamenting, covered/hid themselves before him.
segments.s242-g45: This is a vocative/exclamatory address. که‌ای here reflects the older poetic vocative 'که ای' (O/ah), followed by a string of epithets: شاه 'king', پیروز 'victorious' (adjective after the noun, a common poetic order), جاوید 'everlasting'. زی is the imperative form of زیستن 'to live' (an exhortation: 'Live!').
segments.s242-g46: This is a dependent clause starting with که 'that/so that', giving a wish or further address: خود 'yourself/your very person' (emphatic), جاودان 'everlasting' qualifies the following noun زندگی 'life'. را marks زندگی as definite/accusative. The final word سزی here is archaic/ambiguous in the poem (context suggests a jussive/optative meaning 'may (you) make/keep/fit' or otherwise 'grant/ensure'), so grammatically it functions as the closing verb expressing a wish for making life eternal.
segments.s242-g47: This clause states origin and purpose. ز شهری = 'from a city' (ز is the poetic 'from' + شهری 'of/city'), به داد آمدستیم is a pithy past-tense phrase 'we came to give/bring justice' (به = 'to/for', داد = 'justice', آمدستیم = 'we came'). دور 'far' modifies the whole clause (we came from afar). Word order is poetic but clear: origin → purpose → agent.
segments.s242-g48: This is an explanatory relative clause introduced by که 'that/namely': ایران 'Iran' is the subject/topic, ازین سوی 'from this side' uses از + این (here shown as the orthographic element ین) + سوی 'side/direction', and زان سوی تور = 'and from that side (is) Tūr' with زان = 'from that' and تور the proper name Tūr. The clause contrasts the two sides.
segments.s242-g401: عماری here refers to the household/retinue associated with a dwelling (poetic use). بسیچید is a past-tense narrative verb 'mustered/assembled'; رفتن is the infinitive 'to go' functioning as the complement/goal of بسیچید. به + راه is a prepositional phrase 'toward/on the road' placed after the infinitive in poetic word order (verb before its complement).
segments.s242-g402: مر is an archaic directional 'to/unto' (here 'toward'); آن خفته را is 'that sleeper' with را marking the definite/affected object; اندر آن جایگاه is a locative phrase 'in/within that place.' The sequence gives the destination: 'toward that sleeper in that place.'
segments.s242-g403: ز یک‌سو is a prepositional phrase 'on/from one side.' نشستنگه is a compound noun 'a seat/place to sit,' and کام را ('the desire' + را) is the definite/affected object—i.e. 'a seat for pleasure/desire.' The object marker را shows this noun phrase is what is being prepared or arranged.
segments.s242-g404: دگر here means 'the other (side)' or 'another'; ساخته is the past participle/verb 'made' (used as a past action). جای آرام را is 'the place of rest' with را marking it as the thing made. The clause thus states that, on the other side, they constructed a calm/quiet resting place.
segments.s242-g405: بگسترد is a past narrative verb 'he/they spread'; it appears before its objects in poetic narration. کافور is the direct object 'camphor' and بر جای خواب is the locative 'upon the bed.' The verb + prepositional phrase describes placing camphor on the sleeping-place.
segments.s242-g406: همی here is an imperfective/continuous marker in classical poetry meaning 'kept / was continually'; ریخت is the past of ریختن 'poured.' بر چوب صندل گلاب is 'rosewater upon the sandalwood' (بر = on, چوب صندل = sandalwood timbers, گلاب = rosewater). The phrase describes a continuing action of pouring fragrance on sandalwood.
segments.s242-g407: چو آمد is the temporal conjunction + past verb 'when he came.' به نزدیک شهر اندرا is a prepositional locative 'to/near the city Andra' (به + نزدیک = toward/near). The clause sets the temporal and spatial scene.
segments.s242-g408: بپوشید is a past-form verb with the b- prefix meaning 'he/she covered' or 'drew (a cover).' بر خفته is the phrase 'over the sleeper' (target of the covering) and بر چادرا is 'with the veil' or 'over the veil'—poetic word order puts these prepositional phrases after the verb. The verb thus takes the sleeper as the one covered by the veil.
segments.s242-g9: This is a prepositional + relative clause: به + noun introduces location ('at/to a ...'). که introduces the relative clause that describes that noun. یک روز بگذشت is a duration clause ('a day passed'), and آب at the end is the head noun of that relative clause (literally: 'the water that a day passed'). In older/poetic Persian the noun described by که can occur after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s242-g10: نسازد is the negative form (ن‑ + سازد) used here in the sense 'does not befit / is not proper' (an archaic/poetic use of ساخت/سازیدن), followed by the subject خردمند. ازو ('from it') marks source, and جای خواب ('place of sleep') is the noun phrase that would have been made. Word order is verb — subject — source — object, which is acceptable in classical Persian.
segments.s242-g11: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بهری ز گیتی is a phrase 'a/because of a turn/benefit from the world' (بهر here = benefit/turn, followed by ز + گیتی 'from the world'). برو گشت is a poetic two-word verbal sequence (برو + گشت) meaning 'turned/departed', and راست is an adverb 'straight/directly', giving the sense 'turned away straightaway.'
segments.s242-g12: که introduces the subordinate/reason clause 'for/because'. کین means 'vengeance/hatred' and is the object of the following verb. همی is the old continuous/affirmative particle ('indeed/still'), باز means 'again', and خواست is the past tense 'he desired/sought' — together 'for he indeed sought again vengeance for Siavash.'
segments.s242-g13: به + بگماز: به introduces location or purpose ('at/to the bā-gamāz'); بگماز here is a noun meaning a place/appointed seat (or assembly) and بنشست is the past 'sat down'. یک روز شاد is a time/quality phrase 'one joyful day' modifying the sitting (i.e., he sat for one joyful day).
segments.s242-g14: ز introduces a genitive/partitive relationship ('of/from'), so ز گردان لشکر = 'of the army's divisions/battalions'. همی is the continuous/affirmative particle, کرد is the verb 'made/did' and یاد is the noun 'remembrance' — the compound همی کرد یاد means 'he kept/caused remembrance', i.e. 'he remembered' or 'he called to mind'.
segments.s242-g15: به introduces the material or attire ('in brocade'). دیبا is 'brocade/silk'. بیاراسته here functions as a descriptive adjective meaning 'adorned/arrayed' (note: the provided senses list an opposite reading; in context it describes the king's dress). گاه is a temporal/occasional word ('at the time/occasion') and شاه is the subject 'the king'.
segments.s242-g16: نهاده is the past participle/perfect 'put/placed'. به سر/بر both mark location 'on (his) head' — classical Persian often uses such prepositional phrases in sequence. کیانی کلاه is a noun phrase 'Kayanian cap/crown' (modifier + noun). The whole phrase means 'he had placed the Kayanian cap/crown on his head.'
segments.s242-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. کیخسرو (proper name) is the subject; آمد is simple past 'came'. به + noun + infinitive (به کین خواستن) expresses purpose/intention — 'came to seek vengeance' or 'with the aim of vengeance'. Word order is slightly inverted in poetry but functions as a single temporal clause.
segments.s242-g2: جهان ساز نو is a compressed, poetic phrase meaning 'to make the world new' (جهان = world; ساز here as 'maker/device' forming a verbal-noun compound). خواست is past 'he wanted/wished' and is followed by the infinitive آراستن 'to adorn' as the action he desired. The clause thus states his wish to remake the world and to adorn it.
segments.s242-g3: ز + place (ز توران زمین) = 'from the land of Turan'. گم شد is a passival/intransitive perfect-like construction 'was lost' (گم = lost, شد = became/was). آن تخت is the affected noun 'that throne'. و گاه ('and, then / at a time') introduces the next temporal clause; the poetic order places location and verb before the noun.
segments.s242-g4: برآمد is intransitive past 'arose/appeared'. به خورشید here gives a likeness or source — 'with/like the sun' (i.e. sun‑like radiance), and بر تاج شاه is the locative phrase 'upon the king's crown' (بر = on, تاج = crown, شاه = king). The clause describes light or splendor rising onto the crown.
segments.s242-g5: بپیوست is past 'joined/was joined'. با شاه ایران is the accompanying phrase 'with the Shah of Iran'. سپهر (the heavens/sky) appears as the topical noun and is the entity that joins — poetic inversion places the subject after the prepositional phrase.
segments.s242-g6: بر آزادگان = 'upon the free / the nobles' (prepositional object). سپس the verb بگسترد (prefixed with بر) 'spread/extended' appears before the noun مهر 'affection/kindness', so the clause reads 'spread affection over the free'. The repeated بر is a poetic reinforcement of the locative/privative marker.
segments.s242-g7: زمانه چنان شد = 'the times/fortune became such'. که introduces a clause of comparison/explanation 'that/which', بود از نخست = 'as it was from the beginning' (بود = was, از نخست = from the first). Overall: the situation returned to its original condition.
segments.s242-g8: به آب وفا is a prepositional phrase 'with/in the water of fidelity' (به + noun expresses instrument/means). روی خسرو is a possessive phrase 'Khosrow's face' (noun+name in apposition) and بشست is simple past 'he/it washed'. Poetic word order places the instrument first and the verb at the end; the natural reading is 'Khosrow washed his face with the water of loyalty.'
segments.s242-g17: نشسته is a past‑participle/adjectival predicate 'seated'. به گاه اندرون is a prepositional phrase giving place/time ('in/at the inner chamber'); the preposition به + گاه is a classical locative/temporal construction. می‌به چنگ is a poetic juxtaposition ('wine and harp' or 'with wine and the harp') where می‌به is an archaic poetic pairing (here indicating wine) and چنگ is the instrument (harp) present with the sitter.
segments.s242-g18: دل و گوش‌داده is an elliptical nominal phrase: دل ('heart') joined by و to گوش‌داده ('having given ear' = attentive/listening). به آوای چنگ is the complement introduced by به meaning 'to/toward' and names the target of attention ('to the melody/sound of the harp').
segments.s242-g19: به رامش نشسته is another adjectival/clausal phrase: به + رامش 'in/with tranquility' modifies نشسته 'seated'. بزرگان 'the great ones' is the subject, and به هم ('with each other' / 'together') marks that they are sitting collectively.
segments.s242-g20: A short appositive/name-list: فریبرز کاوس lists proper names in apposition to 'the great ones', while با introduces the companion(s). گستهم here functions as a proper name (Gostaham) following با 'with'.
segments.s242-g21: چو at the start is the comparative/illustrative particle 'like/such as' introducing a list of names (گودرز, کشواد, فرهاد, گیو). The nouns are simply enumerated with و 'and'; the verb is omitted by ellipsis, taken from the surrounding clause.
segments.s242-g22: A second Χو phrase repeating the same comparative sense 'like/as': چو + a list of names. گرگین and میلاد are proper names here; شاپور نیو is a name plus an epithet/occupation (نیو = ney, the reed‑flute), so 'Shapur the ney‑player.'
segments.s242-g23: شه نوذر pairs the title 'king' (شه) with the proper name نوذر (Nowzar). آن طوس لشکرشکن is an appositive: آن 'that' points to طوس (Tūs) and لشکرشکن is an epithet 'army‑breaker' attached to him.
segments.s242-g24: چو ... و چون repeats the comparative particle 'like' before names (رهام, بیژن). چون here is synonymous with چو ('like/as'). رزم‌زن after بیژن is an epithet meaning 'battle‑striker' or 'warrior', characterizing Bijan.
segments.s242-g417: چنین گفت = "thus said"; this is a formulaic introductory clause ('چنین' = 'thus/so' + 'گفت' = 'he said'). 'کای' is a vocative particle ("O") addressing 'کردگار' (the Creator). 'ار' starts a conditional ('if'), and 'مرا' is the object clitic ('me'), split here into 'م' + 'را' with 'را' the definite/object marker.
segments.s242-g418: This clause gives the conditional consequence. 'رهایی' = 'release/freedom' is the object; 'نخواهد' here functions as a future/auxiliary meaning 'will not' (negated future). The form 'بدن' in the text is an archaic/graphical variant (contextually 'to give') but the provided senses list doesn't include that meaning, so it is irregular here. 'ز' = 'from' and 'ایدرا' is an archaic adverb ('thereupon/this place').
segments.s242-g419: 'ز' here marks the person concerned ('with regard to' or 'as for'); 'گرگین' is the proper name Gorgin. 'تو خواهی' is 2nd-person present/future ('you will/would want/choose'). 'مگر' is adversative ('but/rather' or 'however' in this context) and introduces the contrary notion; 'کین من' = 'my hatred/vengeance' (possessive order: noun + possessive pronoun).
segments.s242-g420: 'بر او' = 'against/upon him' (preposition + pronoun). 'بشنوی' is 2nd-person present-subjunctive/imperative form of 'شنو' used here as 'you will hear' or 'you shall hear.' 'درد و نفرین من' is a coordinated object: 'my pain/sorrow and my curse' (constructed noun + possessor); word order places the possessed nouns before the possessor pronoun.
segments.s242-g421: 'که' introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('that/for/because'). 'او' is the subject ('he'). The sequence 'بد مرا بر بدی رهنمون' is literal: 'an evil (بد) guide (رهنمون) for me (مرا) toward evil (بر بدی)'; word order is somewhat inverted (object clitic split as 'م' + 'را' in the edition). 'بر' before 'بدی' carries the sense 'toward/to' here.
segments.s242-g422: 'همی خواند' is a habitual/present classical construction ('he continually/keeps on reciting'). 'خواند' here means 'recites' or 'chants'; 'بر من' = 'over me' (on/upon me); 'فراوان فسون' = 'many enchantments/spells' with adjective before noun. The whole expresses repeated magical action directed at the speaker.
segments.s242-g423: 'منیژه بدو گفت' = 'Manijeh said to him' with indirect object 'بدو' ('to him'). 'دل شاد دار' is an imperative/instructive formula: 'keep (دار) your heart (دل) glad (شاد)'; 'دار' here works as an imperative 'keep/hold.' Word order is verb-final in the clause that follows the reporting verb.
segments.s242-g424: This is a wish/formula: 'همه کار نابوده را' = 'all ruined/lost deeds' (noun phrase with postpositive adjective + object marker 'را'). 'باد' is an optative particle ('may' — a wish), and here combines with 'دار' (an archaic/poetic verbal form) to express the hope/wish 'may they be (so)' or 'let them be gone.'
segments.s242-g153: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The phrase Bižan + به بیشه marks the person and the direction ('Bijan into the thicket'), and برافگند is a classical/poetic third-person past of برافکندن ('to cast/throw off'), taking چشم as its object—so the clause literally reads 'when Bijan cast (his) eye into the thicket'. Word order is slightly inverted compared with modern colloquial Persian because of the epic style.
segments.s242-g154: بجوشید is a third-person past/inchoative of جوشیدن used here in the emotional sense 'to seethe/gush (with feeling)'; its inversion (verb before خونش) is emphatic. خونش is خون + possessive -ش 'his blood'. به تن پر ز خشم is a compact descriptive phrase: به + تن ('in/into the body'), پر ('full (of)') + ز ('from/of', poetic از) + خشم ('anger'), i.e. 'his body full from anger'.
segments.s242-g155: The repeated گرازان (literally 'boars, boars') is an emphatic or vocative-style repetition common in epic diction. نه آگاه ازین is a negated predicate: نه (not) + آگاه (aware) + ازین (from/of this), meaning 'not aware of this'.
segments.s242-g156: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that...'. نهادست is an archaic/epic third-person form meaning 'is placed/has been set' (here 'is seated' or 'has set himself'); بر marks the surface ('on') and بور qualifies زین ('the fair/pale saddle'), so the clause says 'that Bijan is placed on the fair saddle.'
segments.s242-g157: به marks the indirect object 'to Gorgin' (the addressee). The sequence میلاد گفت places the speaker (Milad) before the verb گفت ('said'); اندرآی is a classical imperative/command 'enter' or 'come in' used as direct speech. So the clause reads 'Milad said to Gorgin: "Enter!"'.
segments.s242-g158: وگرنه is 'otherwise'. ز یکسو is a short prepositional phrase ('to/at one side') with poetic ز = از. بپرداز is an imperative (second-person) form meaning roughly 'move/perform (make)'; جای is 'place'. Together they form the instruction 'otherwise, move aside to one side' (literally 'otherwise, from one side make place').
segments.s242-g159: برو is the imperative 'go'. تا here introduces the goal/extent ('until' / 'as far as') and is followed by the locative phrase به نزدیک آن آبگیر ('to near that pond'), where به + نزدیک marks motion toward a nearby point and آن is the demonstrative 'that'.
segments.s242-g160: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause 'من با گراز اندر آیم' uses با for accompaniment ('with') and اندر آیم (classical verb form) 'I enter / I come among' (آیم here = 'I come' in a classical register). به تیر uses به as an instrumental 'with/by (means of)' indicating the weapon; تیر is 'arrow'. The whole means 'when I come among the boars with an arrow'.
segments.s242-g209: ز بهر ... is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning “for the sake of…”; ز is a literary preposition comparable to از/به meaning “for/because of,” followed by بهر + noun to express purpose or motive. The middle word وز here functions as an archaic conjunctive link (essentially “and for”), so the whole chunk reads literally “for the sake of increase and for the sake of name (fame).”
segments.s242-g210: به راه = “on/along the path” (به + noun forms a locative/goal phrase); جوان is the possessor/qualifier “the young (man).” بر بگسترد دام is the verbal core: بگسترد is a classical perfective/past-form of گستراندن “to spread/lay out,” with the prefix بـ found in older usage; بر is the preposition “upon/on” linked to گستردن, and دام is the object “trap.” Word order places the locative phrase before the verb, yielding “he spread a trap upon the youth’s path.”
segments.s242-g211: نگر is an imperative ‘look/see/consider.’ تا introduces an indirect question here together with چه (‘what’/‘how’), so تا چه = “to see what” or “see what.” بد is an adverb/qualifier “badly/evil(ly)” modifying ساخت; ساخت is the past of ساختن used here in the sense “did/acted” (classical usage where ‘ساخت’ = ‘performed/contrived’). آن بی‌وفا is the noun phrase “that unfaithful one” (subject) following the verb, a common Classical Persian word order inversion.
segments.s242-g212: مر is a dative/clitic meaning “to/for me” (the narrator’s perspective). او را is the definite object/topic marker construction “him” (او with را). چه is the interrogative “what”; پیش آورید (پیش آوردن) literally “to bring forward/produce/inflict” is used here to mean “to cause/inflict,” and از جفا gives the source/quality: “of cruelty/injustice.” Together this asks “what cruelties did he bring upon him (to/for me)?” with the earlier subject (آن بی‌وفا) supplying the actor.
segments.s242-g213: بدو is a dative phrase “to him” (به + او contracted), placed at the start for emphasis. آن زمان = “that time/then” (time adverbial). مهربانی نمود is the verbal phrase “showed kindness” where نمود is the past form of نمودن ‘to show/do,’ taking the noun مهربانی as its object: “he showed kindness.” The explicit subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s242-g214: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase “with kindness/well” (به + noun used adverbially). مر is again the dative clitic “to/for me” (marking the narrator’s perspective), followed by او را (the direct object “him” with را). فراوان ستود is the verb phrase “praised abundantly” (فراوان as adverb, ستود past of ستودن ‘to praise’). Word order places the dative before the object for emphasis: “he praised him abundantly before/to me.”
segments.s242-g215: چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” از جنگ و کشتن = “from/after war and killing” (از marks separation/source). بپرداختند is a past-tense plural form meaning here “they ceased/laid aside” or “they were done with” (classical usage: to give up or leave off an activity). The clause means “when they stopped the fighting and slaughter.”
segments.s242-g216: نشستنگه is a noun ‘place of sitting/assembly’ (a meeting/seat). رود here is the musical instrument rud (classical lute), not a river; in epic contexts رود often names a string instrument. و ties items in a list, and می‌ساختند (mi-ساختند) is the imperfective/past-habitual “they would make/compose/play” — with ساختن used in a musical/compositive sense here. So the clause lists peaceful, social activities after battle: they set up the assembly and made/played music on the rud.
segments.s242-g137: از آنجا is a locative prepositional phrase 'from there'. بسیچید is a past (3rd sg.) poetic verb 'mustered/gathered'; in epic Persian the verb often precedes the subject, so بسیچید بیژن reads Verb + Subject. به راه is a directional phrase 'for the road / onto the way', marking his departure.
segments.s242-g138: کمر بست is an idiomatic past action 'he girded his loins / tied his belt' (object کمر precedes the verb for poetic effect). و links the clauses. بنهاد is an archaic past of نهادن 'to place/put', here meaning 'placed'; the phrase بر سر کلاه has inverted word order (more usual is کلاه بر سر نهاد) so the verb comes before the prepositional phrase in this poetic line.
segments.s242-g139: بیاورد is a past tense 'brought'. The verb precedes its subject here (typical poetic inversion): بیاورد گرگین = 'Gorgin brought'. میلاد را is the definite direct object marked by را (Milad is the one brought).
segments.s242-g140: هم‌آواز here functions as an attributive noun/adjective 'companion' or 'one who goes together with'; it forms an appositive description of میلاد. ره را و فریاد را are two definite objects linked by و, specifying 'companion of the road and of the cry/shout' (poetic ordering with را after each noun).
segments.s242-g141: برفت is an epic/poetic past 'he went/departed' (here the provided lemma senses don't match the verbal function, so treated as the narrative verb). از در شاه = 'from the king's gate' where در is 'gate' and شاه is the possessor (poetic juxtaposition). با یوز و باز 'with cheetah(s) and falcon(s)' indicates accompaniment/instrument (the hunting animals).
segments.s242-g142: به + noun + کردن is a common construction forming a purpose infinitive: به نخچیر کردن = 'to go hunting' (کردن here is the light/auxiliary verb verbalizing نخچیر). براه دراز is an adverbial phrase 'on/along the long road' (براه 'on the road/en route', دراز 'long' follows in poetic order).
segments.s242-g143: همی رفت is an iterative/habitual past ('kept going / continued to go'), a common epic narrative form (همی + past). چون پیل introduces a simile 'like an elephant'. کفک افگنان is a participial descriptive phrase: کفک ('little foam') with افگنان (a participial/causative sense 'throwing/producing'), so together 'spraying/throwing up foam' — a vivid participle modifying the manner of going.
segments.s242-g144: This is another participial/manner phrase parallel to the previous one: سر گور و آهو = 'the heads of onager and gazelle' (سر heads precede the nouns, linked by و). ز تن 'from the body' uses poetic ز = 'from'. برکنان is a participial/verb form 'removing/tearing off' (here describing his violent action as he goes).
segments.s242-g385: This phrase describes the cloth laid out: 'به' is the prepositional marker introducing the location or target ('on/onto'); 'دیبا' (brocade) is the noun that names the fabric; 'زمین کرده' is a compound past participle (کرده used with زمین) meaning 'laid on the ground' or 'spread on the earth'; 'طاووس رنگ' is a nominal phrase where طاووس (peacock) modifies رنگ (color) to mean 'peacock‑colored'. Word order is poetic: location marker + material + verb‑participle + color‑phrase.
segments.s242-g386: 'ز' here means 'of/from' and introduces the material or ornament ('made of'); 'دینار' names the coin/wealth used as decoration; the repeated 'دیبا' again names brocade as material; 'چو' is the simile particle 'like/as'; 'پشت پلنگ' ('the back of a leopard') is the image compared to the cloth. The whole segment qualifies the brocade by material and by a simile (pattern like a leopard's back).
segments.s242-g387: The correlative 'چه ... چه ...' enumerates alternatives or lists luxurious items ('whether ... or ...'). The first 'چه' is followed by 'از' ('of/from') to mark the material/source: 'مشک و عنبر' (musk and amber). The second 'چه' introduces the second pair 'یاقوت و زر' (ruby and gold). This is a poetic listing device rather than a conditional.
segments.s242-g388: 'سراپرده' (inner chamber/behind the curtain) is the noun subject; 'آراسته' is a past‑participle adjective meaning 'adorned' (used predicatively); 'سربه‌سر' is an adverbial/qualifier meaning 'entirely' or 'throughout'. The order is noun + adjective + adverb, indicating the whole inner tent was lavishly decorated.
segments.s242-g389: 'می‌سال‌خورده' is a fused adjective/noun meaning 'veteran drinker' or 'seasoned tippler' and functions as the subject. 'به جام بلور' is a prepositional phrase: 'به' here marks the object/recipient of the lifting ('to/with a cup') and 'جام بلور' is 'crystal cup' (جام = cup, بلور = crystal). Together they mean 'the veteran tippler with a crystal cup'.
segments.s242-g390: 'برآورده' is a past participle meaning 'raised/lifted up' (literally 'brought up'); 'با' means 'with' introducing accompaniment or instrument; 'بیژن' and 'گیو' are proper names. 'زور' (strength) follows and can be read as an appositive epithet ('Giv the strong') or as a noun coordinating with 'با' ('with Bijan, Giv, and strength') — the phrasing is compressed and ambiguous in poetry. Most naturally it conveys that the cup was raised with Bijan and with Giv's might/strength.
segments.s242-g391: The temporal phrase 'سه روز و سه شب' ('three days and three nights') opens the clause. 'شاد بوده' is the predicate: 'شاد' (merry) with the auxiliary/past participle 'بوده' -> 'had been merry'. 'به هم' ('together') is an adverbial phrase placed after the predicate to indicate joint merriment. Word order is time → predicate → accompaniment.
segments.s242-g392: 'گرفته' is a past participle/adjectival verb meaning 'seized' or 'taken' (i.e., 'was seized'); 'بر او' ('upon him') marks the person affected; the noun cluster 'خواب مستی ستم' stacks three nouns/qualities where 'خواب' (sleep) is the head, qualified by 'مستی' (drunkenness) and 'ستم' (oppression/injustice) — poetically 'an oppressive, drunken sleep'. This compact noun phrase is the thing that 'گرفته' applies to him.
segments.s242-g217: نبد is an archaic negative past copula ('was not', from نبود) placed before the subject for poetic effect. بیژن آگه is the subject + adjective 'Bijan (was) aware', but because of نبد it means 'Bijan was not aware'. ز + کردار اوی is a genitive construction 'of/ about his conduct', with ز = 'of/from' and اوی the third-person possessive 'his'.
segments.s242-g218: همی is an intensifier here ('indeed/utterly'); راست qualifies پنداشت as 'true' (so 'believed to be true'). پنداشت is used as a past-tense lexical verb 'thought/believed' (sense: 'imagined, thought'). گفتار اوی is the object 'his speech/words', with اوی again the possessive 'his'. The clause means 'he believed his words to be true.'
segments.s242-g219: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; خوردند is past tense third-person plural 'they ate' (here describing an action that befell the group). زان 'from/of that' marks the thing eaten, سرخ is the adjective 'red', and می‌اندکی is an adverbial phrase 'a little/for a short while' modifying the eating.
segments.s242-g220: به + گرگین is the prepositional phrase 'at/toward Gorgin' (گرگین is a proper name here). نگه is a noun 'a look/glance', کرد is the simple past 'made/did' (so 'cast/paid a look'), بیژن is the subject 'Bijan', and یکی 'one / a single' quantifies نگه: 'Bijan cast a single glance at Gorgin.' Note the verb-before-subject word order typical of epic style.
segments.s242-g221: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت here introduces direct speech). چون دیدی is a temporal clause 'when you saw' (چون = 'when'), این جنگ من = 'this battle of mine' (این = 'this', جنگ = 'battle/fight', من = 'my'). The clause introduces the reported question about the addressee's observation.
segments.s242-g222: بدین گونه is a fixed phrase 'in this way/manner' (ب here functions as the prepositional prefix + دین/this forming 'بدین'); گونه 'manner' clarifies it. با خوک is 'with the boar' (با = 'with', خوک = 'boar/pig'), آهنگ من = 'my aim/intention' (آهنگ used as 'intention/plan'); together the phrase asks why the addressee treated the matter concerning the boar in that manner.
segments.s242-g223: چنین داد پاسخ is a set phrase 'thus gave answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = past 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). که‌ای here introduces the direct speech/vocative ('that is: O...'), and شیرخوی is a compound adjective 'lion-hearted' used vocatively — 'O lion-hearted one.'
segments.s242-g224: به گیتی is the locative phrase 'in the world' (به = 'in/at', گیتی = 'world'); ندیدم is first-person past negative 'I have not seen'; چو is the comparative 'like/as', linking to تو جنگجوی 'you (are) a warrior' — the clause asserts there is no warrior in the world like you.
segments.s242-g105: من is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I'. آیم is the present/subjunctive form of آمدن used here as a simple first‑person present/future 'I come / I will go'; it governs the following prepositional phrase. به فرمان این کار is a prepositional phrase 'at/by the command of this affair' (به = 'by/at' with فرمان 'order/command' and این کار 'this task/deed'). پیش at the end is an adverb meaning 'forward / before' that modifies آیم (i.e. 'I will go forward' or 'I will go before').
segments.s242-g106: ز بهر (poetic variant of از بهر/به‌خاطر) means 'for the sake of' or 'because of' and introduces the beneficiary phrase تو 'you'. دارم is the present tense 'I have / I hold' used here in the sense 'I give / I offer' as part of the formulaic phrase دارم تن و جان خویش (my body and soul). تن و جان is the coordinated noun phrase 'body and life/soul'. The final خوی + ش is the older split of خویش 'own/his', with ش the pronominal clitic 'his/own' attached.
segments.s242-g107: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. بیژن is the subject of the subordinate clause; چنین گفت is 'said thus / said this'. The following noun phrase گیو از کران ('Giv from the bank/edge') is the beginning of the main clause and places Giv at the edge/shore (از = 'from/of', کران = 'edge, bank'), the verb for that clause appears in the next segment.
segments.s242-g108: نگه کرد is a compound perfect/simple past: نگه (a noun/Verbal noun 'gaze, look') + کرد 'did' → 'he looked / he fixed his gaze'. و connects the clauses. آن کارش is 'that deed/affair of his' (آن = 'that', کار = 'deed', ش = pronominal clitic 'his'); آمد گران is a small predicate clause where آمد 'came/happened' plus the predicative adjective گران 'heavy' yields 'became heavy / proved burdensome'.
segments.s242-g109: نخست is the adverb 'first / at first'. آفرین کرد is the past tense 'he praised / offered praise' (آفرین here = 'praise/acclamation' + کرد 'did'). مر is the archaic clitic meaning 'me / to me' (here 'on my behalf' or 'before me' depending on context). شاه را is the definite object 'the king' with را marking the object.
segments.s242-g110: به بیژن is the indirect object 'to Bijan' (به = 'to/for', بیژن the proper name). نمود is the past of نمودن 'showed / revealed' (here 'showed'). آنگهی is a temporal adverb 'then / thereupon'. راه را is the definite object 'the way / the road' (را marks it); overall: 'then he showed the way to Bijan.'
segments.s242-g111: بفرزند is a fused form equivalent to به فرزند 'to the son' (the preposition به attached to فرزند), marking the indirect object/recipient of گفت. گفت is the simple past 'he said'. این جوانی ('this youth') is the noun phrase being questioned, and چراست is an archaic fused interrogative 'چرا + است' meaning 'why is it?' or in this context 'what is this? / what is the meaning of this?'.
segments.s242-g112: به نیروی خویش is an instrumental/agent phrase 'by his own strength' (به = 'by', نیروی = 'force/strength' in the ezāfe form taking the following possessive, خوی + ش = older split of خویش 'own/his'). این گمانی is 'this conjecture / this supposition' and چراست repeats the interrogative 'why is it?', so the clause asks why anyone would suppose the matter is due to his own strength.
segments.s242-g225: Literally 'to Iran and Turan to‑you ally is‑not'. The preposition به introduces the domains ('in/for Iran and Turan'); ترا is the clitic pronoun 'to you' written here as ت + را (object marker). یار is the thing that 'does not exist' (نیست), so the whole means 'you have no ally in Iran or Turan.'
segments.s242-g226: چنین کار is a noun phrase 'such a deed/task'; پیش تو is a prepositional phrase 'before you / in your presence / for you' and دشوار نیست is the predicative phrase 'is not difficult'. The adjective دشوار comes before the final copula نیست as typical in Persian word order (predicate at the end).
segments.s242-g227: دل بیژن is the subject 'Bijan's heart'; از گفت او is a causal/agentive prepositional phrase 'from / because of his saying (his words)'; شاد شد is the past-tense compound 'became glad' (شاد + شد). Note classical idiom: feelings are expressed as 'دل ... شد'.
segments.s242-g228: به سان = 'like/as' introduces a simile; یکی سرو is an indefinite NP 'a cypress' (سرو used metaphorically for a tall handsome person); آزاد شد is 'became free/straight/upright' — here a poetic image meaning he stood up or became proud/upright. Verb شد is past tense 'became'.
segments.s242-g229: Word order: به بیژن ('to Bijan') marks the indirect object; چنین گفت (literal 'said thus') is the verb phrase in the past tense; پس پهلوان places the subject 'then the champion' after the verb — poetic V‑S order. پس functions as 'then' (temporal).
segments.s242-g230: که‌ای here functions as a vocative construction (׀که ای׀ → 'O ...'); نامور is an epithet 'renowned/famed'; گرد is an archaic/poetic imperative/optative 'become' (from گردیدن/گشتن) used with an adjectival complement; روشن‌روان is a compound epithet 'bright‑souled / radiant in spirit'. Together this addresses Bijan: 'O famed one, become bright‑souled.'
segments.s242-g231: برآمد is a past verb 'arose/occurred' or 'befell'; ترا (t + را) is the clitic 'to you' with the object/topic marker; این‌چنین کار چند is the noun phrase 'this kind of deed, (on) several occasions' — چند here quantifies (poetically placed after the noun). The structure means 'such deeds have occurred to you.'
segments.s242-g232: به نیروی is an instrumental phrase 'by the power of ...'; یزدان is the divine agent 'God' (genitive: 'the power of God'); و بخت بلند coordinates an additional instrumental 'and high fortune' (بخت بلند = 'lofty fortune/fate'). The whole phrase modifies برآمد to show means/reason.
segments.s242-g297: بپوشید is a past tense verb (3rd-person plural narrative) meaning 'they clothed/arrayed (him)'; the verb takes the following nominal phrase as its direct object. رخشنده is an adjective 'radiant/bright' modifying قبای, and رومی specifies the style/origin of the robe (Byzantine/Rûm). Word order is verb — adjectives — noun (verb precedes its object, a frequent pattern in poetry).
segments.s242-g298: ز introduces source/attachment 'from' (poetic usage) and governs تاج ('the crown'); اندر is an archaic/prepositional adverb 'in/within'. آویخت is past tense 'hung' (3rd-singular) where the thing that hangs follows (پر همای = 'the Homa's feather'). پر همای is a genitive-like phrase 'feather of the Homa' functioning as the object of آویخت.
segments.s242-g299: نهادند is past tense (they placed/set) used transitively; بر پشت 'on the back' is the locative prepositional phrase marking where something was placed. شبرنگ is an adjective 'variegated/mottled' describing زین (saddle); the adjective follows the locative phrase but still modifies زین.
segments.s242-g300: کمر is 'waist' or 'girdle' (here the belt that encircles the waist). خواست is the past tense 'wanted/appointed' used elliptically: the dressers (implicit subject) provided or set up the belt; با introduces an instrumental phrase 'with/using', پهلوانی qualifies the manner ('heroic' or 'appropriate to a champion'), and نگین is the jewel decorating the belt. The whole phrase therefore says they fitted the waist with a jewel-bearing, hero-like belt.
segments.s242-g301: بیامد is past tense 'he/it came' (3rd-singular). بنزدیک is a prepositional/adverbial compound meaning 'near' or 'close to'; آن بیشه 'that grove' is the place approached. شد here is the narrative auxiliary/completive 'became/was' completing the motion clause (a common poetic sequence: verb + locative + شد).
segments.s242-g302: دل is the subject 'heart'; کامجویش is a possessive/relational adjective 'his desirous/pleasure-seeking' (کامجو + -ش = 'his desire-seeking'), so 'his desirous heart'. پر اندیشه شد is a predicative sequence: پر 'full (of)' + اندیشه 'thought' + شد 'became' — together 'became full of thought'.
segments.s242-g303: به زیر is a locative phrase 'under / beneath'. یکی is the indefinite 'one/a certain'. سر وبن is a poetic pairing 'head and stem/root' (سر و بن) describing two parts rising together; in the manuscript form the conjunction appears attached (وبن). شد بلند is the past verbal phrase 'became / rose up tall' (شد + adjective بلند). Overall: 'beneath it one head and stem rose up tall.'
segments.s242-g304: که تا is a subordinating conjunction pair 'so that / in order that'; ز آفتابش = 'from its sun' (z = from, آفتابش = its sunlight). نباشد is a negative subjunctive/jussive form meaning 'may not be / there be no' (used to express prevention), and گزند is the object 'harm'. The clause therefore gives the purpose/result: 'so that no harm may come from its sun.'
segments.s242-g265: اگر is the conditional particle 'if'. ما is the explicit first‑person plural subject 'we'. به نزدیک is a prepositional/locative phrase ('to/towards near') — here meaning 'near to' — followed by the demonstrative آن and the noun جشنگاه 'festival-place'; together this chunk means 'if we come near that festival'. Word order is typical poetic Persian: preposition + locative before the noun phrase it modifies.
segments.s242-g266: شویم is the present-subjunctive/optative form of شدن ('become/come') used with نزدیک to mean 'we approach/come near'. و links to the following verb. بتازیم is the present-subjunctive of تاختن with the old verbal prefix بـ, meaning 'we rush / make haste'. یک روزه راه is a postpositive adjectival phrase 'one-day (road)', i.e. 'a day's journey' (here indicating they would cover the road in one day).
segments.s242-g267: بگیریم is the hortative/present-subjunctive 'let us take / let us seize'. ازیشان = از + ایشان 'from them', indicating source. پری چهره is a compound adjective/noun phrase 'fair‑faced' or 'fairy‑faced (women)'; چهره is 'face' and چند after the noun means 'a few / several' (classical placement: noun + چند = 'several of ...'). Together: 'let us seize from them several fair‑faced (ones)'.
segments.s242-g268: بنزدیک is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'near, close at hand'. خسرو here refers to the king; ارجمند is an epithet 'esteemed / noble' modifying خسرو. شویم is again the subjunctive/present form of شدن meaning 'we come / draw near'. Poetic word order allows the epithet ارجمند to follow, so the whole means 'we draw near the noble (esteemed) Khosrow.'
segments.s242-g269: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گرگین is the proper name Gorgin (the speaker). چنین گفت = 'said thus' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said'); the following بیژن جوان is the nominative phrase 'young Bijan' that will serve as subject of the next verb. Note the clause division: 'When Gorgin said this, young Bijan ...' — the speaker and reportive verb precede the actor.
segments.s242-g270: بجوشیدش is the past form of بجوشیدن with the enclitic pronoun -ش 'him/it' (literally 'it boiled for him'); idiomatically it means 'he boiled over / seethed (with emotion)'. آن is emphatic 'that'. گوهر پهلوان is an appositive epithet 'jewel (or gem) of champions' praising Bijan; placed after the verb as a poetic epithet. Overall: 'young Bijan boiled over, that gem among heroes.'
segments.s242-g271: گهی is an adverb 'sometimes'. نام here means 'name' in the sense of 'reputation / fame', and جست is the past stem (here 'sought' or 'strove for'). اندرَان (classical compound) means 'in/therein' or 'among them', and گاه 'time / at that moment'. کام is 'desire' or 'pleasure'. The line sets up an alternation: 'sometimes he sought fame there, at other times desire.'
segments.s242-g272: جوان is 'the young (man)'. بد + جوانوار is a poetic adverbial cluster: جوانوار 'youthfully' and بد here functions as an adverbial modifier giving a sense of rashness/'ill‑advisedly' — together implying 'rashly/youthfully'. برداشت is the past of برداشتن 'he lifted / took', used with گام 'step' to mean 'took a step'. The clause therefore says the young man, impetuously/youthfully, took a step.
segments.s242-g97: نهاد is the past-tense verb 'placed/put' or 'stepped/placed himself' used here as a simple past (3rd singular). از میان is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'from among / out of', میان functioning as 'middle/among'. گوان is a poetic/archaic word meaning a group/throng here (poetic diction). پیش پای is a locative phrase 'before the feet (of...)' with پیش 'in the presence of / before' placed before پای 'foot/feet'.
segments.s242-g98: ابر شاه is a compound epithet 'mighty/lofty king' (adjective-noun order). کرد آفرین is the verb phrase 'made/exclaimed “afarin” (an acclamation)'; کرد is the light verb 'did/made' and آفرین is the acclamation/object. خدای following the verb phrase is the subject in inverted poetic order: 'God (subject) exclaimed “afarin.”'
segments.s242-g99: که introduces a wish-clause or relative/blessing 'that' / 'may it be that'. جاوید is the adjective 'eternal'. بادی (here) is the optative form ('باد' used as 'may be') and functions as an archaic/poetic optative; this specific optative reading is not among the provided noun senses. The sequence 'و پیروز و شاد' simply strings adjectives with coordinating و 'and' to amplify the blessing.
segments.s242-g100: سرت ('your head') is the noun سَر + 2nd-person suffix; سبز is an adjective 'green/prosperous'. باد here is the optative particle 'may it be' (the optative use of باد). و joins the next clause: دلت is دل + ت (your heart), where ت is the 2nd-person suffix attached to دل; پر ز is the phrase 'full of' (پر = full, ز = 'of' in poetic register), and داد here is the noun 'justice' (not the verb 'gave').
segments.s242-g101: گرفته is a perfect/past participle used as a finite past 'he took/held' (literally 'having taken'). به دست is the common locative/instrumental 'in (his) hand' (به + hand). اندرون جام می places the object 'جامِ می' (cup of wine) after اندرون 'inside' in inverted poetic word order — overall: 'he took (it) into his hand, the cup of wine.'
segments.s242-g102: شب و روز is a fixed adverbial 'night and day' (continuous action). بر یاد is the prepositional phrase 'on/for the memory of / remembering'. کاوس is the proper name 'Kay Kāvus'. کی here is the interrogative/relative particle 'who' (used elliptically/poetically to attach the idea of 'Kay Kāvus' to the memory clause). Taken together: 'night and day in remembrance of Kay Kāvus.'
segments.s242-g103: که again introduces the blessing-clause. خرم is an adjective 'joyful, glad'. به مینو is the prepositional phrase 'in heaven' (مِینو = heaven). بود is the copula 'was' (past-form used here in a benedictory/blessing sense), and جان تو ('your soul') appears after the verb in inverted, poetic order (verb before subject).
segments.s242-g104: به گیتی is the locative 'in/to the world'. پراکنده is the adjective/participle 'scattered / spread' that modifies or predicates the state of فرمان تو ('your command/order'). فرمان تو is a noun + possessive 'your command' (تو follows the noun as the possessive). The phrase expresses the wish that his command reach/spread across the world.
segments.s242-g393: چو here functions as a conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. هنگام رفتن is a noun phrase 'the time of going/departure' (هنگام + infinitive رفتن). فراز آمدش is a light verb construction where آمدش ('came to him') takes فراز as a poetic adverbial/label meaning 'upon/above' or 'rose' — together meaning 'the hour of departure came upon him.'
segments.s242-g394: This is a second clause in which به دیدار بیژن is the prepositional phrase 'to a meeting/visit with Bijan' and نیاز آمدش is an impersonal/experiential construction 'a longing/need came to him' (آمدش = 'it came to him'). Word order is poetic but functions as 'a longing to see Bijan came to him.'
segments.s242-g395: بفرمود تا introduces an indirect command or wish 'he ordered that...' (بفرمود is a dignified past tense verb). تا here marks the subordinate clause purpose/result 'that/so that.' داروی هوش‌بر is a noun phrase 'the mind‑stunning medicine' where هوش‌بر is a compound adjective modifying داروی.
segments.s242-g396: آمیخت is a past tense verb 'mixed' and takes an agent or instrumental phrase introduced by با. Here با نوش‌بر means 'with/by the cupbearer' or more naturally 'into the cup/drink by the cupbearer' — in context the potion is mixed into the drink by the server. پرستنده appears as an attributive/agentive noun near the verb (poetic word order); its exact nuance is ambiguous in isolation (see senses).
segments.s242-g397: بدادند is the past plural 'they gave/granted.' مر is the dative/clitic-like element 'to me' (placed before the objects for emphasis). بیژن گیو را are the given persons, with را marking the definite/direct object; the sequence reflects poetic word order where objects and appositions can be juxtaposed.
segments.s242-g398: This repeats the dative 'مر' ('to me') for emphasis. آن نیک‌دل نامور is a noun phrase 'that kind‑hearted, famous (one)' modifying نیو, with را again marking the object. In context نیو appears as a proper/poetic name or epithet (sense uncertain), so the whole phrase means 'that kind‑hearted, renowned Niu was also given to me.'
segments.s242-g399: منیژه is the subject name. چو introduces a temporal clause 'when' (چو = 'when'); بیژن is the object seen. دژم روی is a compound attributive phrase 'grim/sullen face' (دژم modifies روی), and ماند is past 'remained' or stative 'was' — together 'when Manijeh saw Bijan's grim face' (poetic compression of the verb of perception).
segments.s242-g400: پرستندگان را is the definite object 'the worshippers' marked by را. بر خویش is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward herself' (بر = 'toward/on', خوی + ش = 'one's own' with ش as the possessive clitic). خواند here is a past tense verb meaning 'called/summoned' (to call someone to come).
segments.s242-g161: بدانگه is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that moment' that opens the clause. که introduces the subordinate clause ('when/that'), and the verbal phrase از بیشه خیزد خروش literally orders 'from the grove rises a cry' — خیزد is a present/factual verb (third-person singular) placed before its noun خروش (a poetic inversion: verb then subject).
segments.s242-g162: تو is the explicit second-person address; بردار is an imperative 'take!' commanding the addressee. گرز is the direct object. The conjunction و links the two imperatives/commands, and به جای آر هوش is an idiomatic clause: به جای 'in place' + the (archaic/poetic) imperative آر 'put/arrange' (from آراستن) taking هوش 'awareness, wits' as its object — i.e. 'set/ready your wits.'
segments.s242-g163: به بیژن places the indirect object ('to Bijan') at the start for emphasis; چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' گفت is the reported-speech verb ('said'), but the supplied sense list lacks the verbal sense (see note). گرگین is the proper name 'Gorgin' and گو is a verbal/participial element 'saying'/'said' used with the name — together marking the speaker (poetic word order places the speaker after the clause).
segments.s242-g164: که introduces the content clause ('that/that which'). پیمان is 'pact/treaty' and نه is the negation (here placed after the noun: 'پیمان نه این بود' = 'the pact was not this'). بود is the past copula 'was.' با شاه نو is a prepositional phrase 'with the new king' (با + noun + modifier), so the whole subordinate clause states that the pact in question was not with the new king.
segments.s242-g165: تو is the explicit second-person subject and برداشتی is the past tense, second-person singular 'you took/removed.' The list of objects 'گوهر و سیم و زر' is a coordinated noun phrase ('jewel and silver and gold') following the verb; Persian often places the verb before a series of direct objects in poetry.
segments.s242-g166: تو بستی is another past-tense clause ('you bound/tied/girded'). بستی is the past of بستن meaning 'to bind/gird.' مرین here is best read as a proper name/vocative (addressing someone or naming the addressee). رزمگه را contains the definite object marker را on 'r͟azmgah' (battlefield/arena), and کمر is 'belt/waist' — the phrase means you 'girded the war-belt' (word order is poetic: object phrase appears before the head noun 'کمر').
segments.s242-g167: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' بیژن is the subject, شنید is the past verb 'heard,' and این سخن is the direct object 'these words/this speech.' خیره شد is a compound predicate: خیره (staring/astonished) + شد 'became' = 'became stunned/was struck dumb' — typical Persian copular construction.
segments.s242-g168: همه modifies چشمش ('all his eyes' = 'his whole sight' / 'his vision'). از روی او uses از with روی plus a pronoun to indicate cause or relation ('because of him / from his presence' or more loosely 'because of it'), تیره is an adjective 'dark,' and شد is the past 'became' — so the phrase describes his sight/demeanour darkening as a result.
segments.s242-g89: به + noun phrase here marks the material/garment used ('in/with'); 'دیبای رومی' is a noun phrase 'Byzantine brocade' (دیبای = brocade/silk, رومی = Rûm/Byzantine). 'بیاراستند' is the archaic/literary past tense, 3rd-person plural of آراستن (آراستن → بیاراستن) meaning 'they adorned/arrayed'. The verb follows the prepositional object (verb–object order is common in poetic Persian).
segments.s242-g90: بسی here functions as a quantifier 'many/much' (literary use). ز marks source 'from the assembly' (ز + انجمن). نامور is an adjective used substantively as 'the renowned ones'. خواستند is past 3rd-person plural of خواستن 'they asked/summoned/called for' — here 'they summoned'.
segments.s242-g91: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb گفت. گفت is the past 3rd-person singular 'said' (here used before the subject — verb‑initial order is common in narrative poetry). پس is a temporal/sequence adverb 'then'. شهریار زمین is a compound title 'king (شهریار) of the land/realm (زمین)' functioning as the subject placed after the verb for stylistic/poetic effect.
segments.s242-g92: که‌ای is a vocative/address construction: که + ای (ای as the vocative particle) yielding something like 'O ...' (used to call or address someone). نامداران is the plural substantive/adjective 'the famed/illustrious (men)'. با آفرین literally 'with afarin' — آفرین here is a noun 'acclamation/praise', so با آفرین = 'with applause/praise'. Together this is an address: 'O famed ones, with acclamation...'.
segments.s242-g93: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'whoever/that who'. جوید is the literary present/future 3rd-singular of جستن 'seeks/wishes' (classical present used for general/habitual action). به marks purpose/benefit 'for/to' and آزرم من = 'my honour' (آزرم used here as an abstract 'honour'); رنج خویش = 'his own suffering' where خویش (split here into خوی + ش) functions as a reflexive/possessive 'one's own'. Order is typical: verb then prepositional phrase then object.
segments.s242-g94: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'from that time on/thereafter'. کند is the literary present/future 3rd-singular of کردن 'he will make/does make'. گنج من is 'my treasure' (direct object) and گنج خویش repeats 'treasure his‑own' — an appositive/reflexive construction meaning 'will make my treasure his own'. The doubled noun (گنج ... گنج ...) is emphatic/clarifying in classical style; خوی + ش again yields the reflexive possessive 'his own'.
segments.s242-g95: کس here, together with هیچ, forms a negative indefinite 'no one' (کس ... هیچ ... نداد = 'no one gave any answer'). از انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'from the assembly'. پاسخ نداد is past-negative meaning 'did not give an answer' (نداد = 'gave not' in classical narrative — the negation is conveyed by the combination کس...هیچ and the verb form).
segments.s242-g96: مگر is the exception particle 'except'. The following three words form the proper name/title: بیژن گیو فرخ‌نژاد (a heroic name, here treated as a single referent). Grammatically this yields: 'No one answered, except (for) Bijan Giv Farrokh‑nēzhād.'
segments.s242-g313: کلاه (kalaah) is the head-noun 'helmet/hat'. تهم پهلوان appears as a name/title following the noun without an explicit ezafe (a common poetic omission): 'the helmet of Tihm/Tahm the champion'. بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'on his head' where بر = 'on' precedes سر 'head' and -ش is the enclitic possessive 'his'. Word order: Noun + possessor/title + locative phrase.
segments.s242-g314: درفشان is an adjective/participle 'shining, glittering' describing the helmet. ز introduces the material/source 'from' (poetic form of از). دیبای رومی is a genitive-like phrase 'Roman brocade' (دیبای = brocade/silk; رومی = of Rûm/Byzantium). برش is a noun 'a cut/piece' (i.e. a piece cut from that brocade) referring back to the helmet's ornamentation; the whole phrase says the helmet glittered from (was made of/trimmed with) a piece of Roman brocade.
segments.s242-g315: به پرده درون is a locative expression 'into/within the curtain' (به ... درون emphasizing location). دخت is the noun 'maiden' and پوشیده روی is a compound adjectival phrase 'with her face covered' (پوشیده = hidden/covered used as adjective modifying روی 'face'). Note the poetic omission of ezafe and the adjective-after-noun order.
segments.s242-g316: بجوشید is a past/inchoative verb meaning 'boiled/gushed' used metaphorically for emotion ('his love seethed'). مهرش is 'his love' (مهر + possessive -ش). دگر شد is 'became different' (دگر = other; شد = became). به خوی is a prepositional phrase 'in/with regard to disposition/nature' (خوی = disposition), so the line says his love surged and its character changed.
segments.s242-g317: فرستاد is simple past 'sent'. مر (old/poetic form of 'مرا' or 'مِر' here) functions as the indirect object 'to me' placed before the direct object (flexible word order). دایه را is the direct object 'the nurse' marked by را. چون + نوند is a parenthetical simile/descriptor: چون = 'like/as' and نوند is uncertain here (it may be a proper name or a reference to the musical mode Navand); the phrase likely qualifies the manner/identity of the nurse or her arrival — the exact nuance is ambiguous in the manuscript/lexicon.
segments.s242-g318: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'that/who'. رو here is the noun 'face' (رو) and زیر آن شاخ سرو بلند is the locative 'under that bough of the tall cypress' (زیر = beneath, آن = that, شاخ = bough/branch, سرو = cypress, بلند = tall). Together the clause means 'who(se) face was under that tall cypress bough' — i.e. sent so the nurse could see who was there.
segments.s242-g319: نگه کن is an idiomatic imperative 'look!/watch!' formed from نگه 'gaze/watch' + imperative کن 'do'. که introduces the content 'that/that which follows'. آن ماه دیدار is a poetic epithet 'that moon of the meeting' (ماه used metaphorically for a beautiful person). کیست is 'who is (it)' — کی = who, ست = is (copula).
segments.s242-g320: سیاوش is the proper name 'Siavash'. مگر here functions as a rhetorical interrogative particle 'could it be that...?/is it perhaps...'. زنده is the adjective 'alive'. شد‌گر is a poetic/compound form built from شد (become) + -گر (agent/formative) suggesting 'one who has become' or 'come to be' (here used in a verbal-nominal poetic sense 'has become alive / risen'). پریست in ordinary lexicon means 'worshipper/devoted', but in this context it likely evokes 'پری' (a fairy) + -ست or functions poetically to pose the alternative 'or is (he) a fairy?' — the exact lexical nuance is dialectal/poetic. The line poses a surprised question: is this Siavash returned to life, or some supernatural being (a fairy)?
segments.s242-g121: چنین گفت is a narrative phrase meaning "thus he said" (چنین = thus; گفت = said), placed before the vocative. کای is an archaic/vocative particle meaning "O" (often used addressing kings), followed by شاه پیروزگر, a postpositive epithet construction (شاه = king; پیروزگر = "victorious"/epithet). Word order is typical classical Persian: report verb + vocative + title.
segments.s242-g122: This clause is an imperative prohibition addressed to the king. تو = "you" (subject), بر من = "toward/about me" (prepositional phrase showing the target of the suspicion), به سستی = "of weakness" (به + noun = 'concerning/with respect to weakness'), گمانی مبر = negative imperative "do not take/harbour a supposition" (گمانی = 'a supposition/suspicion', مبر = 'do not take' where مبر is classical negative imperative of برداشتن/گرفتن). The pieces combine to mean 'do not suppose me weak.'
segments.s242-g123: تو = 'you' (address); این گفته‌ها = 'these words/statements' (object). از من = 'from me' (source-marker). اندر پذیر (پذیر with اندر) functions as an imperative or exhortation 'be receptive/accept' — literally 'receive/accept within.' The order (object before verb) is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s242-g124: جوانم is a first-person predicate 'I am young' (جوان + -م copula). ولیکن is a formal adversative 'but.' به اندیشه پیر literally 'old in thought' (به + اندیشه = 'in regard to thought/mind'; پیر = 'old'), so the contrast reads 'I am young, but (I am) old in mind.' The omitted copula before پیر is normal in Persian: 'I am young, but in thought old.'
segments.s242-g125: منم is emphatic 'I am' (اَم form). The sequence of proper names 'بیژن گیو' identifies the speaker (two names placed in apposition), and لشکرشکن is a postpositive epithet 'army-breaker' (compound noun functioning as title). This kind of name + epithet cluster is common in epic self-identification.
segments.s242-g126: سر خوک را is the definite direct object marked by را ('the pig's head'). بگسلانم is a first-person present/future/capable-form verb meaning 'I will rend/sever/tear apart' (ب- prefix + گسلانم). ز تن is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the body' (ز = from; تن = body). The verb comes at the end, with object before it, as expected in Persian.
segments.s242-g127: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بیژن is the subject (Bijan). چنین گفت = 'said thus/this.' شد شاه شاد is a result clause: شد = 'became' and شاه شاد = 'the king (was) happy' (predicate following the auxiliary 'شد'). This is a straightforward temporal-result construction: 'When Bijan said this, the king became glad.'
segments.s242-g128: برو is an imperative quoted or reported ('Go!'). آفرین کرد is a verbal phrase 'he praised/exclaimed "bravo"' (آفرین as exclamation or praise + کرد 'he did/expressed'). و = 'and'. فرمانش داد = 'he gave his command' (فرمانش = 'his command' with possessive suffix referencing the king; داد = 'gave'). Together: he commanded 'Go', praised him, and issued the order.
segments.s242-g305: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase placed before the noun phrase and means 'near/toward'; آن خیمه is a demonstrative + noun 'that tent'; خوب‌چهر is an epithet ('fair‑faced') in apposition to the (omitted) subject. Classical Persian often omits a copula, so this whole phrase functions as a circumstantial phrase marking place.
segments.s242-g306: بیامد is simple past 'came' (third person); the grammatical subject is omitted and picked up from the preceding epithet. بدلش here functions adverbially 'in return / as a consequence' (poetic use), اندر is a locative 'in/within', افروخت is past of افروز 'kindled/ignited', and مهر (object) is 'love/affection'. Note the poetic word order: the verb افروخت appears before its object مهر (inversion for rhythm).
segments.s242-g307: همه دشت = 'the whole plain' (همه emphasizes entirety). ز introduces the source or cause ('from, because of') and governs آوای ('the voice/sound of'); رود و سرود are coordinated ('river' and 'song'). This is a causal/setting phrase: the plain, by reason of the river's voice and the singing...
segments.s242-g308: روان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker (here 'to the moving one / the soul'); همی + داد is an iterative/continuous past construction ('kept giving' / 'continually gave') — همی marks ongoing action in classical Persian; گفتی is a noun meaning 'spoken word/utterance' and درود is the content ('greeting'). The literal order 'روان را همی داد گفتی درود' places the verb داد before the noun phrase گفتی درود (poetic inversion) but means 'continually gave the greeting utterance to the soul/traveler.'
segments.s242-g309: منیژه is the proper-name subject. چو here is the conjunction 'when'. از خیمه is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the tent'. کردش is کرد + the enclitic ـش (object/remote clitic) meaning 'made it / directed it' — here 'کرد (کردن) + نگاه' = 'directed (her) gaze'; نگاه is the noun 'gaze/ glance'. So the clause literally says 'when Manijeh from the tent made (her) glance', i.e. 'when Manijeh looked out.'
segments.s242-g310: بدید is simple past 'saw'. The following noun phrase is the object: آن (demonstrative 'that') + سهی قد (three words: سهی 'dark/dusky' as an adjective, قد 'stature/height' as a noun) + لشکر پناه is a compound 'refuge/protector of the army' (لشکر in genitive relation). Together they form 'that dark‑statured refuge/protector of the host' which is what she saw.
segments.s242-g311: به رخسارگان is a locative phrase 'on/at the cheeks' (به marking direction/place). چون is the simile marker 'like/as'. سهیل یمن is a poetic epithet 'Suhail of Yemen' (a bright star used as a beauty simile). The whole phrase sets up a likeness: 'on the cheeks, like Suhail of Yemen...'.
segments.s242-g312: بنفشه گرفته is a participial phrase: بنفشه here denotes the violet color/quality and گرفته (past participle of گرفتن) means 'has taken' or 'is tinged with'. It modifies the following noun دو برگ سمن = 'two leaves of jasmine' (برگ = leaf, سمن = jasmine). So literally 'two jasmine‑leaves had taken violet (hue)'.
